author: Catherine Bennett
title: cross_topic_1/test/0/test_90
body: Why do intelligent, highly qualified women freely choose to work in the City of London? Future anthropologists, having established that the women were neither transported there nor compelled by a shortage of alternative habitats, may conclude that the attraction was money. Or excitement. Or the opportunity to spend 14 hours a day among men whose emotional development was arrested during a rugby tour of 1987. Whatever the motive, no one can now doubt that the environment is brutish and sexist, although not, presumably, quite as bad as the police, fast-food chains, men’s magazines, mini-brothels or the Church of England Synod. We know this thanks to the steady procession of discrimination and unfair-dismissal claims which has exposed the City’s more traditional customs: bullying, spitting, lapdancing, sexual insults and the rewarding of women with salaries so enormous that their subsequent suffering can only be expressed in multiples of £1m. Without these instructive cases, investment banks might still be able to pose as equal-opportunities employers, committed to diversity, dignity and the promotion of any woman capable of pretending not to hear “hot totty” across a crowded trading floor. The modern City martyr probably descends from Nicola Horlick, whose celebrated 1997 dispute with Morgan Grenfell established that even an argument about staff-poaching could, with sufficient press interest, be elevated into a moral crusade. Horlick, already known as Superwoman, understood that martyrdom is more effective when reporters can be induced to follow the martyr to Frankfurt. Accordingly, the press accompanied her there and back, entranced by the spectacle of a glamorous fund manager confronting bankers on foreign soil. There were, happily, no lasting effects. Horlick recovered with impressive speed, founded other businesses and remained available for interviews. But her pilgrimage showed what could be achieved when City unpleasantness was combined with a large salary, photogenic indignation and lawyers. Successors have since demanded progressively larger sums in recognition of injuries ranging from name-calling to being taken to lapdancing clubs, an experience which, in less lucrative occupations, employees may have to survive without counsel. Claire Bright, formerly of HBOS, where she earned £600,000, sought £11m after allegedly suffering under a man she called “a mini Hitler”. Given the disparity in size between a City office and Nazi Germany, a mini-Holocaust was presumably the most he could have managed. Still, it is difficult not to feel for a person obliged to endure bullying while receiving only £50,000 a month. Money, as everyone knows, cannot buy happiness. It can, however, buy several homes in which to be unhappy, together with expert advice on the correct financial value of the unhappiness. Even Ms Bright’s claim appears restrained beside the £800m collectively demanded by six former Dresdner Kleinwort Wasserstein bankers. The alleged offences included one claimant being described as “the Pamela Anderson of trading” and another witnessing a chief executive date his assistant. Eight hundred million pounds may sound excessive to anyone unfamiliar with the psychic cost of seeing a colleague go out with somebody. But perhaps this only proves how coarsened the rest of us have become. If six bankers really require £800m to recover from such experiences, what compensation could possibly suffice for workers who are routinely insulted, pawed or intimidated for £18,000 a year? The answer, unfortunately, is not £800m. In the non-City compensation system, where the victims are less accomplished at monetising distress, the relevant sums tend to shrink. Victims of the July 7 bombings who lost limbs could receive, from the Criminal Injuries Compensation Board, around £50,000 for a leg. Evidently, one should hesitate before comparing unlike injuries. The loss of a leg in a terrorist explosion is one thing; being called Pamela Anderson at work is another. But even allowing for the unique delicacy of the City nervous system, £800m represents an arresting number of legs. Perhaps newspapers should decline to advertise these spectacular demands until they bear some relation to what tribunals are likely to award. The figures function less as claims than as promotional balloons, floating above the proceedings with the name of the claimant’s lawyers prominently attached. They may also distress the still-traumatised, by which is meant not only bombing victims who have been offered £50,000 for permanent disability, but any legal team forced to contemplate settlement at less than the price of a small sovereign state. It is reassuring, naturally, that the City has discovered moral language. Institutions once associated with greed can now speak earnestly of respect, inclusion and psychological safety, generally shortly before instructing counsel to explain why the alleged victim was incompetent, unstable or insufficiently committed to the team. There is no hypocrisy here. Only a diversified portfolio of principles. Can schools be protected from the forces of darkness? A Guardian advertisement placed by Oasis UK, the charity founded by the Rev Steve Chalke, sought an “accomplished fundraiser” to realise a vision of “outstanding schools and community hubs” rooted in Christian values. The successful candidate would cultivate, among other benefactors, “HVDs”. This turns out not to mean highly volatile disciples, but “high value dealers”, a phrase whose faintly narcotic character should not prejudice us against its use in Christian education. Neither the fundraiser nor, apparently, Chalke needs to have run a school. This is no objection, because the government has decided that experience of education may actually inhibit the imaginative management of children. Schools are now best entrusted to amateurs: religious fanatics with unusual ideas about homosexuality; self-promoting tradesmen; aspiring barons; parents; classroom assistants; and anyone who has recently announced that they want “to give something back”. Peter Hyman, once Tony Blair’s speechwriter, went to Islington Green school “to do something different”. There is something touching about the confidence with which a political communicator, having helped explain government policy, can move on to children, rather as a bored television chef might try ceramics. The children, unlike a ministerial speech, cannot be rewritten overnight, but this is presumably part of the challenge. Then there is Reg Vardy, the car dealer whose sponsorship of a school was accompanied by enthusiasm for creationism. In older educational systems, knowledge of biology might have been considered useful to someone influencing science teaching. Under the new arrangements, a strong opinion is just as good, particularly if supported by money. What, after all, could trained educationalists know about schools that is unavailable to a determined cleric, motor trader or former spin doctor? Teachers may understand classrooms, discipline, learning difficulties, curricula and adolescence, but amateurs possess vision. They establish hubs. They cultivate HVDs. They promise transformation. Above all, they arrive unsullied by the defeatism that comes from knowing what the job entails. Churches once claimed moral authority over children because they understood eternity. Businesses claim it because they understand targets. Politicians claim it because they understand neither, but have met an inspirational sponsor at dinner. Between them, and with the assistance of a fundraiser who knows his high value dealers, how could children’s minds be anything but safe? This week Reading Naomi Wolf’s The Treehouse produced an unexpected desire to revisit Little House in the Big Woods, where Pa’s advice on life and cabin construction always seemed more dependable. He could build a door, smoke venison and explain winter without once proposing a transformational community hub. More shamefully, I enjoyed George Galloway’s self-destruction on Celebrity Big Brother. It is not often that moral grandiosity receives so prompt and complete a punishment. Nor, regrettably, that the punishment involves pretending to be a cat.

author: Catherine Bennett
title: cross_topic_1/train/0/train_2
body:
The new issue of *Prospectus*, evidently completed during that distant phase of British history when Sir Malcolm Vane had not yet lost two safe seats, been impersonated by a lettuce on *The Last Leg*, or recorded a personal approval rating normally associated with Japanese knotweed, offers an inspiring account of the Conservative leader’s “late flowering”. At 68, we learn, Vane has “finally grown into himself”. He is “lighter, calmer, liberated from the need to impress”; a political Benjamin Button who, having begun as an elderly junior minister in 1997, is now progressing backwards towards the radiant infancy required of a modern party leader.

The cover portrait is particularly hopeful. Vane, tieless, sits on a kitchen table in what the caption describes as his “surprisingly relaxed family home”, although the mug beside him bears the logo of a Westminster thinktank and the kitchen belongs, according to the estate agent’s particulars, to somebody in Wandsworth. “I’m an optimist,” he says. “People are ready to listen.”

They may have been when he said it. Magazine journalism, unlike the common mayfly, has a long and dignified gestation. Interviews must be transcribed, themes identified, photographs colour-corrected, cover lines focus-grouped and references to Benjamin Disraeli checked by an intern. By the time a statesman’s reinvention reaches the newsstand, the statesman himself may have been reinvented several more times, quite possibly as an independent MP.

Thus *Prospectus*’s “Man for the Moment” arrives in the same week that Vane’s party has lost the Fen Ditton by-election to Reform, the North Bromley by-election to Labour and, in a council contest in Dorset, come fourth behind an independent candidate whose manifesto consisted principally of complaints about a bench. “The bench has been neglected for too long,” the victorious councillor declared. Few Conservatives could disagree.

Still, it would be unfair to blame *Prospectus*. Its profile went to press before Vane’s conference speech, in which he promised to “restore gravity to government” while standing beneath a malfunctioning electronic slogan reading GRAVY TO GOVERNMENT. It preceded his radio interview about food prices, when he guessed that a supermarket chicken cost £1.20. And nobody, certainly not a monthly magazine, could have anticipated his attempt to demonstrate ordinary tastes by naming his favourite television programme as “the one with Claudia Winkleman and the deceivers”.

Only six months ago, newspapers were unanimous about Vane’s gifts. He was “forensic”, said the *Times*. “Relentless”, agreed the *Telegraph*. “Incisive”, contributed the *Spectator*, under a portrait making him look like an unusually suspicious bank manager. Quentin Letts detected “a whiff of the old cavalry sabre”. John Crace, less captivated, thought him reminiscent of a man returning soup in a hotel restaurant. But even sceptics accepted that here, at last, was a grown-up: a leader who had read documents, remembered numbers and could identify Moldova on an unlabelled map.

All three qualities have since undergone their customary political deterioration. Vane’s forensic questioning is now pedantry. His relentlessness is an inability to stop talking. His incisiveness has become rudeness, particularly after he told a nurse on a campaign visit that her account of the NHS was “anecdotally interesting but statistically otiose”. The old cavalry sabre, you gather, has turned out to be a butter knife with something unpleasant adhering to it.

This should not surprise anyone familiar with the party’s leadership system. Every Conservative saviour is selected to correct the defects of the previous Conservative saviour, then discarded for exhibiting defects that make the previous ones look unexpectedly attractive. Boris Johnson’s disorder was cured by Liz Truss’s ideological clarity. Truss’s clarity was followed, after 49 days of national clarification, by Rishi Sunak’s competence. Sunak’s competence prompted an appetite for Kemi Badenoch’s combativeness; Badenoch’s combativeness created the need for Vane’s tranquillity. Now that tranquillity has been reclassified as torpor, members are reportedly attracted to a younger candidate with “energy”, preferably one who has never expressed a recorded opinion.

The pattern is so established that leadership coverage could surely be written in advance. Candidate X is more formidable in private. Friends describe an unexpected warmth. Enemies underestimate a first-class mind. There follows a walk in the Cotswolds, an anecdote about a parent, and a photograph with a dog that does not belong to the candidate. Within months, the warmth is weakness, the mind is over-rated and the dog has joined Reform.

Vane’s admirers insist that he needs time. “You don’t replace a washing machine because one sock comes out damp,” said a shadow minister, developing an analogy that may not have been fully serviced before use. But what if the washing machine is making a terrible noise, flooding the utility room and displaying an error code that nobody can decipher? What if its warranty expired before the first reshuffle? What if the manufacturer, having sent five engineers in eight years, now says the appliance is functioning as designed?

The Conservative party treats leaders much as consumers treat cheap washing machines: admiration at delivery, fury when the programme sticks, then abandonment outside the house with a note saying FREE, WORKS FINE. Johnson, Truss, Sunak and Badenoch are already lined up on the political pavement, each too costly to repair and too recent to seem vintage. Vane, according to *Prospectus*, has discovered “a new ease in his own skin”. It is fortunate, since he may soon have plenty of leisure in which to enjoy it.

His successor’s warranty will be shorter still. The membership, having once allowed leaders years to disappoint them, now expects the full cycle—renewal, triumph, betrayal, removal—to be completed on the rapid-wash setting. Eventually the new leader will be deposed during the acceptance speech, saving everyone the trouble of pretending to like them.

Meanwhile, perhaps the party should consider its environmental responsibilities. Britain cannot go on fly-tipping former Conservative leaders indefinitely. At this rate, even the oceans will shortly contain more discarded prime ministerial material than plastic.

**The radicalising influence of tuck**

The obituaries of Julian Creed, the veteran Marxist journalist, have dwelt tenderly on his lifelong hatred of privilege, less often mentioning that this hatred was cultivated at St Anselm’s, a private boarding school with its own trout stream, armoury and competitive system of internal mail.

Creed credited his politics to the house system. Having been placed in Templeton, whose colour was puce and whose boys were routinely beaten at rugger by Chudleigh, he concluded by 14 that hierarchy was wicked, inherited power indefensible and the means of production urgently in need of seizure. British socialism may owe less to Engels than to cold showers, prefects and the confiscation of toast.

This raises concerns about kinder private schools. If fagging disappears, dormitories are heated and housemasters learn to say “wellbeing”, where will tomorrow’s revolutionary columnists come from? Little Simz has said that her anti-establishment instincts were sharpened at a moderately strict school where lateness attracted detention and jewellery was restricted. Had staff encouraged unrestricted self-expression, she might now be composing jingles for NatWest.

The education secretary’s campaign for competitive sport may therefore have unforeseen benefits. Every child forced into cross-country by a triumphant games teacher is a potential enemy of capitalism. If the minister succeeds, the far left may yet hail her as its greatest recruiting sergeant.

**Your compulsory new best friends**

A report on interracial friendship finds that social integration remains disappointingly incomplete. On examining my own circle, I discover failings of an even graver kind. I have too few black friends, certainly, but also no Welsh friends, estate agent friends, baronet friends, forklift-driver friends or friends who breed alpacas. There is nobody from Stoke, nobody in the diplomatic service and not one close companion who repairs orchestral instruments.

How is the conscientious adult to rectify this? Friendships take time, and employers remain strangely reluctant to offer friendship leave. Perhaps the government could introduce civic mingling days, on which citizens report to leisure centres and are paired with someone demographically improving.

A lottery might be fairer. Every five years, each adult could be assigned 20 new friends, balanced for race, income, region, occupation, age, voting history and attitude to air fryers. Refusal to meet for coffee would incur a fine; repeated failure to remember birthdays, community service.

There would be administrative difficulties. Some people already possess 20 friends and may resist compulsory additions. Others might attempt to swap. Still, random allocation would prevent social prejudice and cure loneliness at a stroke.

Although, as with all lotteries, there must be losers. Somewhere, one innocent citizen would open the official envelope and discover that the state had assigned him Nigel Farage, Laurence Fox and Dominic Cummings.

author: Catherine Bennett
title: cross_topic_1/train/0/train_5
body:
When Susie Orbach launched Antidote, the campaign to bring “emotional literacy” into British public life, its signatories included Tessa Jowell. At the time, this may have seemed an optimistic project. Westminster was not conspicuously overrun with people able to identify an emotion, unless “anger at being found out” counts; nor did the average cabinet minister appear likely to distinguish between shame, grief and the irritation caused by an unsatisfactory Today interview.

But perhaps Antidote should declare victory. Political life is now so emotionally literate that ministers sound less like public servants than marriage counsellors trying to discharge Britain before lunch. Following Sir Geoffrey Plumb’s apology for failing to mention that the prime minister had personally approved several meetings which he had previously said never occurred, the prime minister announced that the apology allowed “everyone to draw a line and move on”.

Within hours his press secretary agreed that “the important thing now is to move on”. The culture secretary, asked whether she had been misled, said she had “achieved closure”. Another minister hoped that the apology would “bring closure to the whole process”. By teatime, the government appeared to be chairing a national encounter group. We had acknowledged the hurt, honoured the journey, closed the process, drawn the line and moved on. All that remained was for the country to lie on a rug, breathe into its stomach and release Sir Geoffrey with love.

It was striking, certainly, that none of the people still asking questions felt either closure or movement. But then, in the therapeutic system now favoured by government, emotional progress is not something the injured party experiences. It is something announced by the person who has just survived an inquiry. “I have achieved closure” means, approximately: I’ve won, so bugger off.

Were one fortunate enough to lie on the prime minister’s couch, one might begin cautiously. “I feel stuck,” one might say. “I was told there were no meetings, then that there might have been informal meetings, then that meetings had occurred but were not meetings in the usual sense, and finally that it was unreasonable to expect anyone to remember meetings which had happened in the past. I feel betrayed. I am finding it difficult to trust.”

The prime minister would incline his head, in the empathetic manner perfected for hospital visits. “I hear that,” he might say. “But the important thing is not to get trapped in a cycle of blame.”

“I also feel,” one might continue, “that the inquiry you promised has been replaced by a review, whose chairman reports to the official whose conduct he is reviewing.”

“We need to move forward.”

“And the documents?”

“We mustn’t reopen old wounds.”

“And Sir Geoffrey’s contradictory evidence?”

“He has apologised.”

“For which contradiction?”

“I sense you are not yet ready for closure.”

In that case, the citizen-patient might profit from Dr Miranda Hope’s Letting Go Without Losing Yourself: Seven Steps Beyond Betrayal. Dr Hope, a relationship specialist and founder of the Moving Forward Institute, explains that fixation on an injury can be “like repeatedly pressing a bruise to see if it still hurts”. The person who injured you, she warns, “may already be getting on with their life”.

How true. Even as the public presses its bruise, the prime minister is getting on with his life, launching the next inquiry. In fact, he has got on with it so successfully that the last inquiry already belongs to what ministers call “another place”, meaning the past, where records become unclear, promises lose their edges and ministerial responsibility is archived owing to pressure on storage.

Dr Hope recommends that, before ending a relationship, the wounded person recall the good times. So let us remember them. There was the good time when the prime minister promised that all meetings would be recorded. The happy afternoon when he said no donor would receive privileged access. The unforgettable evening when the culture secretary guaranteed a “transparent and demonstrably independent process”. Then came the magical weekend when the government promised to publish everything, subject only to removing everything it considered private, irrelevant, confidential, commercially sensitive, security-related or embarrassing.

Could we perhaps make a memory box? Into it would go the original ministerial code, the missing diary page, the press secretary’s first statement and a little photograph of Sir Geoffrey saying he had never met anyone. When feelings of resentment return, we could open the box and remember how young and trusting we all were.

Still, it is curious that politicians should have appropriated counselling language at all. Real therapists, unlike ministers, do not generally tell patients that closure has been achieved on their behalf. They are apt to define moving on as a difficult, non-linear process involving reflection, truthfulness, grief and the rebuilding of trust. None recommends that the offending partner set the timetable, appoint the therapist, redact the case notes, announce that lessons have been learned and leave with the house.

“Closure” is the most abject of counselling clichés, being both mawkish and conveniently indefinable. A door can be closed. So can an inquiry, a railway line or a troublesome department. But a political controversy is not closed merely because the person at its centre has become bored with it. You cannot claim closure on someone else’s behalf, any more than you can forgive yourself in the name of the person you have injured. Though no doubt there is a government seminar working on that.

**The overseer’s overseer**

For a glimpse of emotional literacy’s bureaucratic cousin, consider the appointment of the new BBC chairman. Asked whether the selection panel had been independent, the culture secretary referred to Sir Peregrine Vale, who had supervised the process. Asked whether candidates had been improperly sounded out, she said this was a matter for Sir Peregrine. Asked whether Downing Street had expressed a preference, she explained that Sir Peregrine was “the guarantor of the integrity of the process”.

Who, then, guaranteed Sir Peregrine? This was, the secretary said, to misunderstand his role.

Sir Peregrine is chairman of the Council for Ethical Appointments, deputy chairman of the Public Confidence Partnership, senior independent director of BritVision Holdings and non-executive chairman of Civic Synergies plc. He is also visiting professor of leadership at the University of Wessex, honorary professor of organisational futures at East Mercia Business School, special adviser to the Institute for Responsible Influence, patron of Leaders Without Boundaries and a member of the advisory board of the Centre for Better Boards.

For these and related services, Sir Peregrine receives £94,000 for two days a month from Civic Synergies, £61,500 from BritVision, £27,000 from Public Confidence, £18,000 from the Ethical Appointments Council and an undisclosed sum from Vale Strategic Perspectives, whose clients are confidential for reasons of confidence. His university work is unpaid, except for expenses, accommodation, travel, secretarial support and a modest annual honorarium of £12,000.

He is, therefore, ideally placed to recognise conflicts of interest, having met nearly all of them.

Students of leadership may know Sir Peregrine from his book Altitude: Seeing the Whole Organisation. “A leader,” he writes, “must know when to fly at 30,000 feet, but also when to land and walk among the baggage handlers.” Elsewhere he warns: “You cannot remain in the helicopter forever. Sometimes you must come down, refuel and ask who owns the helipad.”

His celebrated list of “seven unspoken employee requests” includes: “See me”; “Hear me”; “Stretch me”; “Include me”; “Surprise me”; “Acknowledge the greatness within me”; and “Please do not micromanage my becoming.” Managers at Civic Synergies are said to carry laminated copies, presumably so that the greatness within them can be acknowledged even in wet weather.

At an Institute for Responsible Influence dinner, Sir Peregrine concluded with a poem of his own composition:

Life is a circus,  
Leadership the ring,  
Some of us juggle,  
Some of us swing.  
But when the tent is folded,  
And the elephants depart,  
The greatest high performer  
Is the leader with a heart.

There was, according to the institute’s newsletter, sustained applause.

His management philosophy also draws on General Patton, whose principles Sir Peregrine adapted for modern public service. These include “Never ask permission to take responsibility”, “A good plan today is better than accountability tomorrow”, “Lead from the front, unless the front is operational” and the particularly useful: “Don’t let adverse facts stand in the way of a good decision.”

It would be unkind to suppose that this last maxim influenced Sir Peregrine’s supervision of the BBC appointment. It is true that the successful candidate had dined twice with the prime minister, employed the culture secretary’s former adviser and donated to a foundation run by Sir Peregrine’s wife. But these are facts, and therefore potentially adverse.

In any event, the culture secretary has repeatedly assured us that Sir Peregrine is satisfied. Whether anyone should be satisfied with Sir Peregrine is, as she would remind us, entirely a matter for Sir Peregrine.

author: Catherine Bennett
title: cross_topic_1/train/0/train_6
body:
“Of course the cynics will sneer,” the prime minister warned, launching what he called “the Big National Dialogue”, “but I believe the British people are ready for a serious, honest and grown-up conversation about the country we want to become.” Of course. It would have been unsettling had the cynics, on this occasion, applauded, or merely waited to inspect the serious, honest and grown-up conversation before deciding whether it deserved to be sneered at. Happily, by identifying their response in advance, the prime minister relieved them of this responsibility. Sneering was not an assessment of the Dialogue. It was opposition to dialogue itself.

There would be public meetings, for people who enjoy fluorescent lighting and lukewarm urn-water, and an “interactive digital platform” for everyone else. Nothing, the minister for public engagement promised, would be off limits. The government wanted to listen. It wanted to learn. Above all, it wanted to hear “the authentic voice of Britain”, provided that this voice supplied its full name, postcode, email address, age bracket and consent to receive further communications from selected democratic partners.

A week later, the authentic voice was in magnificent form. “Thank you for giving ordinary people like me the chance to say how well things are going,” wrote Melanie Pugh, a “working mother and occasional cyclist” from Bedfordshire. “My family has never been more secure. Under the previous government, we were frightened, divided and cold. Now my daughter has a tablet computer and my husband has been promoted twice.”

Melanie did have one piece of constructive criticism. “Could schools do even more to teach kindness?”

They could certainly try. Elsewhere, Gordon Simpkin of York reported that “after years of misery under the opposition”, his plumbing business was thriving. “I now employ 14 apprentices, all from diverse backgrounds, and recently took my wife to Madeira. Please keep doing what you are doing, but perhaps think about making parking apps simpler for older users.”

Doreen Vass, a retired laboratory assistant from Chelmsford, thanked the government for her bus pass, her new hip and the “general feeling of hope”. She remembered the opposition years all too clearly. “People forget how bad it was. There were holes in the roads and everyone argued. Nowadays you can walk down any high street and see confidence.”

It was encouraging to discover that so many contributors had mastered the ministerial technique of inserting a microscopic complaint into an otherwise seamless testimonial, rather as an advertiser adds “results may vary” to the bottom of a miracle cure. Trevor Nibbs, 63, was delighted with the economy, the NHS, the railways and the national mood, although he wondered whether someone might move the bottle bank in Lower Marden. Yasmin Coles praised the government’s “brave moral leadership” and the unprecedented opportunities enjoyed by her children, before adding that it would be helpful if the number 47 bus stopped nearer her mother’s bungalow. Henry Forth, describing himself as “politically independent but fair-minded”, believed ministers had “restored pride after the bleak opposition decade”. His sole reservation concerned the scarcity of public lavatories near Winchester Cathedral.

Even the wording was curiously harmonious. The country had “turned a corner”. Britain was “moving forward”. Families felt “secure”. Communities were “resilient”. Everyone welcomed the opportunity to contribute “constructively”. If these were indeed spontaneous communications, the government’s achievement was greater than it claimed. It had not merely improved the economy and renewed society. It had taught thousands of citizens to write like junior press officers.

One contribution, from an Arthur Winston of North London, fitted so naturally among the others that it remained on the site for several days:

“In the old days, before the glorious Revolution, London was not the beautiful city that we know today. It was a dark, dirty, miserable place where hardly

author: Catherine Bennett
title: cross_topic_1/validation/0/validation_2
body:
Is the lottery glass half-full, Mr Blair, or half-empty? It is your own favourite method of efficacy-evaluation, so perhaps you can help. If the licence goes to Sir Richard Branson, the glass is half-full of a self-advertising billionaire whose celebrated dislike of profit has somehow left him with an airline, a railway, a record company, a ballooning habit and enough money to keep himself in open-necked shirts for ever. If it goes to Camelot, it is half-empty because the existing, extravagantly rewarded operator, having extracted prodigious sums from the national weakness for brightly coloured balls, has been invited to go on doing so.

Or it may be the other way round. Either way, the contents look undrinkable.

Still, there was one affecting dividend from the National Lottery Commission’s decision to return Camelot to the jackpot. Sir Richard, normally photographed in postures suggestive of a man who has just invented fun, appeared close to tears. Viewers familiar with the death of Little Nell, the sufferings of Little Dorrit and the more elevated passages of Victorian melodrama may have been moved by the spectacle of a disappointed tycoon discovering, perhaps for the first time, that money cannot buy absolutely everything. Only nearly everything. Had a violist been available, or a small orphan to clasp his knee, the scene would have been complete.

It is worth recalling, before we all move on, that Labour once found Camelot almost as disgusting as everyone else did. In opposition, when righteous denunciation cost nothing and could be performed without the nuisance of implementation, Labour politicians were unanimous that the lottery should be run for the people, not for private gain. Tony Blair promised a “people’s lottery”. Chris Smith, before becoming culture secretary and discovering the many advantages of helplessness, said that a Labour government would replace Camelot with a non-profit organisation. Jack Cunningham argued that the lottery ought to be operated on the lines of those in other countries, where, by some strange continental perversion, running a public lottery did not require the enrichment of a small group of executives.

There would be, Mr Blair assured us, a lottery genuinely dedicated to good causes. The people’s money would be returned to the people. The people, in fact, were rarely out of it. They were to own the lottery morally, emotionally and, where convenient, rhetorically, while someone else handled the contracts, bonuses and awkward questions.

This was in the old days, when Labour opposition could identify a profiteer without first appointing a commission to determine whether profiteering represented best value. Camelot’s executives had become famous for their rewards. Their riches offended ministers-to-be. Here, they said, was a public franchise being milked by private interests. It would all be different under Labour.

And so it is. Camelot has been awarded another licence.

In some respects, the lottery debacle is even more impressive than the Dome, with which it shares many of the characteristics of a natural disaster. The Dome, after all, was inherited in embryonic form from the Conservatives. Labour merely embraced it, enlarged it, sentimentalised it, filled it with zones and allowed it to consume public money while ministers explained that nobody could possibly have foreseen what everybody had predicted. With Camelot, there is no such excuse. Labour had opposed the operator. It had advertised an alternative. It knew exactly what it disliked and, on entering office, arranged for the disliked thing to continue.

The two enterprises have, however, one essential feature in common. No one of political consequence will be held responsible. The Dome happened, like fog. The lottery decision unfolded, like weather. Ministers were present, naturally, but only as awed spectators before forces too complex for human direction. Contracts gathered. Lawyers descended. Computer systems became unstable. Commissioners made decisions of such refinement that judges later struggled to distinguish them from abuses of power.

Chris Smith, the culture secretary, has explained throughout that the National Lottery Commission is independent. This is a useful constitutional principle, according to which a minister may appoint an incompetent body, give it a task of national importance, support it when it disgraces itself and then plead that independence prevents him doing anything about it. Independence, in this usage, means freedom from ministerial interference but not, apparently, freedom from ministerial patronage.

Why did Smith appoint a commission so conspicuously deficient in the expertise necessary to award one of the country’s largest commercial licences? Of the commissioners’ many accomplishments, few appeared to involve running lotteries, assessing complicated computer systems or negotiating billion-pound contracts. Perhaps this was intentional. Experts can be such a nuisance. They know things. They ask informed questions. They may even recognise an untenable process before it reaches the high court.

Most mysterious of all was the appointment as chair of Dame Helena Shovelton, whose suitability for presiding over this immense commercial contest appears to have been one of those truths too obvious to require demonstration. No doubt she possessed leadership qualities. Possibly she could fly at 30,000ft, see the overarching picture, drop to 20,000ft, set the direction of travel and acknowledge the greatness within the other commissioners. What she could not do was conduct a lawful competition.

Presented with only two bids, the commission rejected both. Camelot’s proposal was judged unacceptable because of concerns about its computer arrangements. Sir Richard’s People’s Lottery was also judged unacceptable. At this point, ordinary notions of fairness might have suggested that both bidders should be allowed to remedy their failings, or neither should. The commission chose a more creative course. It gave Branson another chance and denied Camelot one.

Even among children this would have been recognised as slightly off. “Both of you have failed, but only Richard may try again” is the sort of adjudication that would have most toddlers reeling with sympathetic indignation. Yet it was approved by the commission and, more remarkably, by the Treasury Solicitor’s department. Perhaps the lawyers, too, were flying at 30,000ft.

Camelot, an organisation with a well-developed appreciation of both profit and litigation, applied for judicial review. It won. Mr Justice Richards described the commission’s conduct as “conspicuously unfair”, “unlawful” and “an abuse of power”. These are not, even allowing for judicial reserve, expressions of mild concern. They do not mean that Dame Helena had chosen the wrong biscuits for a meeting. They mean that the body appointed to guard the integrity of the national lottery had itself behaved with gross unfairness.

One might have expected Dame Helena to resign immediately. Instead, she stayed. Chris Smith supported her. The commission would learn lessons. It would move forward. Its confidence in itself remained, like Camelot’s profits, impressively intact.

When Dame Helena finally departed, her resignation was not attributed to the unlawful abuse of power identified by a high court judge, but to “media vilification”. This was unfortunate. The media, by reporting the judgment, had apparently persecuted a blameless public servant. Had newspapers shown greater restraint, she might have continued indefinitely, serenely chaired above the wreckage, like Mrs Skewton preserving the attitudes of elegance while the house burns down.

The other commissioners remained. They had, after all, only participated in the decision. Under a successor chairman, Lord Burns, they would complete the process. Lord Burns acknowledged that the commission had been discredited, which might seem a powerful argument for replacing it. Unfortunately, he explained, there was no time. Thus the people who had demonstrated their unfitness to decide were uniquely qualified to decide quickly. The invalid jury must return because assembling a competent one would delay the verdict.

There is, admittedly, a practical consideration. Where would ministers find five or six replacement commissioners at short notice? The country may contain thousands of people with knowledge of business, technology, gambling or public procurement, but how many would also possess the indispensable qualification of being known to Chris Smith?

In the absence of political leadership, the lottery has effectively been governed by lawyers. First came the Treasury solicitors, who approved the commission’s original discrimination. Then came Camelot’s QC, who persuaded the court that it was unlawful. Then came the City firm appointed to oversee the commission’s final deliberations, thereby ensuring that no commissioner could scratch her nose without first obtaining counsel’s opinion.

The firm’s previous work for all the interested parties was, naturally, a coincidence of the sort that occurs continually in the close, happy world of City law. If a large firm has acted for Camelot, Branson, the government, the commission, the lottery, several good causes and possibly the numbered balls themselves, this is not a conflict. It is breadth of experience.

Camelot’s appetite for legal action was hardly a secret. Like many organisations whose public image depends on cheques presented to smiling nurses, it becomes remarkably unsentimental when its commercial interests are threatened. Yet ministers established a process so vulnerable that the company needed only to summon its lawyers and point at it.

Given the choice between commissioners who do not understand the process and lawyers who do, why not dispense with the ornamental remnants of political government and let the lawyers run everything? They could award franchises, draft policy, overturn policy, investigate themselves and charge by the hour. Elections would be replaced by judicial review. Instead of manifestos, the parties could publish skeleton arguments. The nation would be governed by clever people in chambers, leaving ministers free to attend openings, praise the people and explain that all controversial decisions were independent.

But perhaps, Mr Blair, this is already your plan. Lords, lawyers, unelected advisers: one by one, they are being installed. The glass is not half-full or half-empty. Counsel is still considering it.

Related
• Camelot wins new lottery licence after legal battle  
• Sir Richard Branson: “The public deserved better”  
• Culture secretary expresses full confidence in revised process  
• National Lottery Commission promises lessons will be learned  
• Lord Burns: no time to replace discredited commissioners

author: George Monbiot
title: cross_topic_1/test/1/test_103
body:
While the newspapers measure the Home Secretary’s love affair in column inches, another country is being measured in corpses. Every furtive meeting, every implausible denial, every tremor in a ministerial marriage is treated as a matter of national consequence. The resumption of civil war in the Democratic Republic of Congo, by contrast, has scarcely disturbed the surface of public life. This conflict has already killed an estimated 3.8 million people. Apparently it requires a better sex angle.

The Rwandan army has again crossed into north-eastern Congo. Rival factions of the Congolese army, some aligned with Rwanda, are fighting each other. Civilians are being murdered, raped and tortured; families are abandoning their homes and fleeing into the forests. Even before this latest assault, the International Rescue Committee estimated that more than 1,000 people were dying every day from the disease and malnutrition left behind by the previous war. Nearly half of them were children under five.

How can the deadliest conflict since the second world war be treated as background noise? Part of the answer is simple: editors believe that their readers are interested only in events they already understand. Congo is complicated, so it is ignored; because it is ignored, it remains complicated. But there is another reason, and it is more disturbing. To acknowledge what is happening there, we must abandon one of the West’s most cherished moral narratives. Rwanda, rightly recognised as the victim of genocide, has become an aggressor. The government that arose from the catastrophe of 1994 has committed some of the gravest crimes of the subsequent decade.

Rwanda’s first intervention in what was then Zaire possessed an apparent justification. After murdering some 800,000 Rwandans, the Interahamwe militias fled across the border. They were sheltered first by Mobutu Sese Seko and then by Laurent Kabila. From their camps in Congo they threatened to reinvade Rwanda and resume the slaughter. Paul Kagame’s forces pursued them, supported Congolese rebels and helped to overthrow Mobutu. Few governments would have tolerated a genocidal army regrouping just across the frontier.

But a legitimate security interest became the pretext for an imperial enterprise. After Rwanda’s second invasion in 1998, the pursuit of the killers became subordinate to the pursuit of minerals. Congo’s diamonds and coltan were funnelled eastwards. A United Nations Security Council investigation found that by 1999 the Rwandan army’s Congo operation was supplying 80% of its military budget: around $320m a year. The war, in other words, was not merely paying for itself. It was financing the institution that prolonged it.

Six African nations joined the struggle for Congo’s wealth. What followed was presented as a civil war, but much of it was a turf war over mines. Millions were displaced. Thousands were forced to dig minerals from the ground or sell sex to the men carrying guns. Towns changed hands because they commanded roads, airstrips or mineral deposits. The civilians who happened to live on top of valuable ore discovered that natural wealth is often indistinguishable from a death sentence.

Rwanda’s operation appears to have been the most efficient. Amnesty International reported that it was directed from Kigali, rather than improvised by soldiers in the field. The formal withdrawal in 2002 did not end it. Troops were absorbed into local forces, commercial networks remained intact and armed proxies continued to secure the mines. The latest invasion looks less like a new war than the removal of an obstacle from an old business plan: militias threatening Rwanda’s mineral interests must be neutralised, while militias serving them are sustained.

Compare the reaction to Congo with the response to Darfur. The suffering in Sudan has rightly generated campaigns, parliamentary debates and acres of coverage. Congo, where the death toll is far greater, has inspired nothing comparable. The distinction cannot be explained by the scale of the crime. It arises partly from the identities we have assigned to the participants.

We are accustomed to seeing the Rwandan Patriotic Front as the good army: the disciplined force of genocide survivors that drove out the killers while the world looked away. To accept that its government could become a perpetrator requires us to hold two ideas in our heads at once. A people can suffer an atrocious crime. A government claiming to represent them can then inflict atrocious crimes on others. Public debate, which prefers heroes and villains to human beings, finds this almost impossible.

Supporters of Kagame suggest that the accusations arise from manoeuvring by hostile powers on the UN Security Council. If so, the conspiracy is unusually broad. Amnesty International, Human Rights Watch, the International Crisis Group and Global Witness have reached similar conclusions. So has a British all-party parliamentary group. So has the US State Department, whose government has hardly been eager to undermine Rwanda. To dismiss all these findings requires not scepticism but devotion.

The evidence does not consist of a few anonymous claims. The reports run to hundreds of pages. They contain testimony from survivors, witnesses and perpetrators; names of officials and victims; dates and locations; estimates of the value of stolen minerals; the identities of companies and buyers. Their collective weight is crushing.

They describe children as young as three being raped. Women who resisted were subjected to genital mutilation. People were forced to work in mines under armed guard; some were buried alive when shafts collapsed or when their usefulness expired. Villages were looted and burned, and those who resisted were killed. Millions were driven into forests where there was no food, medicine or shelter.

Most of the 3.8 million did not die from bullets or machetes. They died from fever, diarrhoea, respiratory disease and hunger. This does not absolve the armies. If you burn a family’s house, steal its food, destroy its clinic and force it into a malarial swamp, you cannot plead innocence when its children die. There is no moral distinction between killing people immediately and creating the conditions in which death becomes inevitable.

One defence, advanced among others by the journalist Victoria Brittain, is that critics have confused Rwanda’s disciplined regular army with the chaotic rebel groups surrounding it. The US State Department’s assessment makes this hard to sustain. It recorded “numerous credible reports” of regular Rwandan troops killing, torturing and raping civilians in Kivu and Maniema.

But even if every atrocity had been committed by a proxy, the distinction would be worthless. Rwanda created and maintained the RCD-Goma militia. It was formed in Kigali, financed by Rwanda and equipped, according to Global Witness and Amnesty International, with heavy weapons supplied by the Rwandan government. Commissioning murder does not become morally preferable to committing it in person. A man who hires a killer cannot defend himself by pointing out that his own trigger finger is clean.

None of this excuses the Congolese government. Kinshasa’s forces and allies have also committed appalling crimes. But the authority of the central government barely extends into the east. A UN report leaked to the BBC identified Rwanda and its proxies as the dominant military power there, controlling most of the mineral trade and participating in nearly all the fighting.

The Interahamwe remains Rwanda’s universal explanation. Yet the militia is now far weaker than it was when Rwandan forces occupied the region. The International Crisis Group points out that Rwanda enjoyed overwhelming military control of eastern Congo between 1996 and 2002, but failed to destroy it. The British parliamentary group found Rwandan troops stationed in areas where there were no Interahamwe at all. In some places, it reported, Rwandan forces or their allies cooperated with the very men they claimed to be hunting, when cooperation helped them control the mines.

If the genocidaires were the overriding concern, Rwanda had both the time and the power to deal with them. Their survival has instead become politically useful. They provide a permanent justification for intervention: an enemy that must be fought but never quite eliminated. The old horror is being used to drag eastern Congo into a new one.

Stopping this need not require an invasion, an occupation or another grand international scheme. Rwanda is a tiny, fragile state, heavily dependent on foreign aid. More than a third of that aid comes from Britain. A government with such leverage could demand the withdrawal of troops and proxies, the dismantling of mineral-smuggling networks and cooperation with international prosecutions. Instead, Western governments issue expressions of concern while continuing to supply the money that permits the war machine to operate.

But governments will act only when silence becomes embarrassing. At present, they have little to fear. The public is studying a minister’s bedroom; the press is counting the phone calls. Congo’s dead possess neither celebrity nor novelty. They vanish without inconveniencing our moral certainties.

Remembering Rwanda’s genocide is essential. Treating it as a perpetual licence is obscene. Victimhood is not a hereditary exemption from judgement. If “never again” means anything, it must apply not only to the crimes committed against those we recognise, but also to the crimes they later commit against others. Nothing will change in Congo until we wake up to this war, and abandon the comforting pretence that yesterday’s victims cannot become today’s perpetrators.

www.monbiot.com

author: George Monbiot
title: cross_topic_1/test/1/test_104
body:
Barbara Probst Solomon, Richard Sennett and a small army of internet polemicists have warned that George Bush’s America represents an emerging fascism: soft fascism if one is feeling cautious, the full jackbooted article if not. The comparison is understandable. An administration that invents enemies, suspends legal protections, glorifies military force and treats dissent as disloyalty will inevitably remind people of the 1930s. But understandable comparisons can still be wrong.

Robert Paxton, in The Anatomy of Fascism, defines fascism as a politics driven by an obsessive sense of community decline, humiliation and victimhood, and by compensatory cults of unity, energy and purity. Fascist movements allied themselves with established elites, but they did not begin as mere instruments of those elites. They rose through hysterical popular mobilisation: marching columns, street violence, mass parties and ecstatic submission to a national leader. Bush does not summon the public into politics. He tells it to shop. His ideal citizen is not the stormtrooper but the consumer: frightened, indebted, politically inert and watching the war on television.

This does not mean that the Bush project is unprecedented. It means that the precedent has been misidentified. What is being revived in the United States is not fascism but Puritanism. Until the left understands this, it will continue to fight the wrong century.

Puritanism is commonly remembered as a joyless cult of prohibition. Its adherents stripped churches, smashed images, closed theatres, persecuted sexual licence and appeared to regard pleasure as evidence in a criminal trial. This seems remote from Republican America, where salvation is advertised between hamburgers and four-wheel-drive vehicles. But the prohibitions were the surface of the doctrine, not its economic core. Puritanism was the spiritual expression of a profound transfer of power.

In early 17th-century England, the surviving institutions of the feudal state performed some of the functions later associated with social democracy. They existed principally to defend the monarchy, aristocracy and established church. Yet they also restrained exploitation. As RH Tawney showed in Religion and the Rise of Capitalism, published in 1926, Charles I’s government sought to nationalise industries, regulate foreign exchange and prevent powerful landowners and merchants from treating the poor as disposable material. It prosecuted lords who evicted peasants, employers who withheld proper wages and magistrates who refused poor relief.

There was nothing democratic about this arrangement. But there was a governing assumption, inherited from medieval Christianity, that economic life was subject to moral law. Property carried obligations. Trade was tolerated, not worshipped. The market was a servant whose conduct had to be supervised.

This system collapsed because a new kind of power had arisen. Over roughly 150 years, international commercial companies accumulated financial resources on a scale Europe had not encountered before. Medieval industrial organisation was subjected to a new money-power. The decisive forces were not initially factories or machines, but commerce and finance. Merchants could move money across borders, lend to governments and dictate the conditions under which monarchs survived. Kings who once regulated financiers became their petitioners and, eventually, their puppets.

In England, the dissolution of the monasteries accelerated the revolution. An immense stock of land and wealth was seized, redistributed and traded by an emerging commercial class. Enclosure drove peasants from common land. Villages were emptied to make room for more profitable enterprises. Land speculation spread, while financial markets developed instruments resembling futures and arbitrage. Fraud, monopoly, insider dealing, political favour and the conversion of public wealth into private fortunes flourished: all the vices we now associate with the Age of Enron.

The first reformers did not bless this process. Early Reformation theologians denounced enclosure, usury, rack-renting and the abandonment of the poor. They preached charity and justice and demanded the punishment of those who exploited their neighbours. Something of this spirit persisted among the first settlers of New England. In England, however, it was crushed alongside the people whose interests it defended.

What survived was a different Reformation. Puritanism became the religion of traders, money lenders, bankers and industrialists. Calvin offered the commercial classes what the old religious order could not: a theology in which their economic activity might be represented not as a regrettable necessity but as service to God. Commerce could become a calling. Capital accumulation could become evidence of obedience. Capitalism could glorify God.

The inversion proceeded by stages. Calvin’s doctrine of individual purification produced what Tawney called an idealisation of personal responsibility before God. This developed into a theory of individual rights. The traditional scheme of Christian virtues, in which the strong owed duties to the weak, was turned upside down. By the middle of the 17th century, many English Puritans had ceased to regard poverty primarily as misfortune. It was evidence of indolence, disorder or moral failure. Wealth, conversely, appeared to reward energy, discipline and will.

Individualism in religion became individualism in morality. Individualism in morality became contempt for the social fabric. If salvation depended upon the solitary soul, and prosperity reflected personal virtue, society could be absolved of responsibility for suffering. The poor had manufactured their own poverty; to relieve them might interfere with God’s judgment.

Religious and commercial callings then merged. In 1684 the preacher Richard Steele explained that the tradesman served God through diligence in his calling. Business success became a token of spiritual grace. Joseph Lee anticipated Adam Smith’s invisible hand by more than a century, arguing that the advancement of private persons served the public interest. As usual, “private persons” meant men of property, while “the public” included those whose advancement they destroyed.

Tawney described the Puritans as early converts to “administrative nihilism”. Economic affairs, they believed, should be settled by businessmen, unhampered by the antiquated morality imposed by church or state. Government should protect property and enforce contracts, but refrain from asking how property had been acquired or what those contracts did to the powerless.

They also devised a useful theory of social obligation: obligation consisted of refusing assistance. Helping the poor encouraged idleness; idleness promoted spiritual dissolution. Cruelty could therefore be renamed moral instruction. The workhouse, like the modern withdrawal of welfare, was an instrument of salvation.

There is one obvious difference between these people and Bush’s Republicans. The Puritans worshipped production, while the Republicans worship consumption. But this is the same disease presenting a different symptom. In both cases economic activity becomes a test of virtue, wealth becomes proof of merit and failure becomes a private sin. Tawney summarised the old Puritan creed: the world exists not to be enjoyed but to be conquered; only its conqueror deserves the name of Christian. Today conquest is measured not only in work performed or territory seized, but in houses, cars, contracts and quarterly returns.

Those who resisted this inversion were dealt with brutally. The Levellers and Diggers retained the radical promise of the Reformation: equality before God, common rights and justice for the poor. They were suppressed by the propertied interests whose revolution they had helped to secure. Moral persecution assisted the process. The pursuit of adulterers and sodomites gave the impoverished classes someone beneath them to despise, while those above them seized the land.

Ronan Bennett’s novel Havoc in its Third Year, set in the 1630s, catches this machinery perfectly. Its society is obsessed with terrorists—Irisher and Jesuit—with homosexuality, sexual licence and the vicious punishment of moral deviance. Public support for the poor is denounced as indulgence. Fear is cultivated, private virtue policed and economic injustice concealed behind spectacles of righteousness. Exchange the black suits for grey ones, and the characters could walk straight into Bush’s America.

Why has this ideology resurfaced in 2004? Because it must. The enrichment of the elite and the impoverishment of the lower classes cannot be sustained by force or fraud alone. They require a story explaining why the winners deserve everything they take and why the losers deserve everything withheld from them. In the United States, that story must be religious. Bush’s government is driven back to Puritanism by historical necessity.

Calling it fascism might satisfy our appetite for dramatic analogies, but it obscures the economic theology that makes the project work. To understand what we are confronting, we must look not to the 1930s but to the 1630s.

author: George Monbiot
title: cross_topic_1/test/1/test_105
body:
There once was a watchdog called Mo,  
Who told the centrifuges: slow.  
He handed out fuel,  
Then cited the rule,  
And wondered where warheads might grow.

Mohamed El Baradei has been given what must be the most preposterous job in international diplomacy. He is required to remove the capacity to make nuclear weapons with one hand, while distributing the materials and expertise required to make them with the other. His predecessor, Hans Blix, was trapped in the same vaudeville act: policeman and salesman, prohibitionist and supplier. Last week Iran exploited this contradiction to dismiss the demands of the International Atomic Energy Agency. It could do so for the simple reason that the agency is seeking to prevent it from exercising the rights the agency was established to promote.

This magnificent muddle began in 1953, with President Eisenhower’s Atoms for Peace speech to the United Nations. It is difficult now to recapture the atomic rapture of the period. Nuclear energy was not merely another means of boiling water. It was the secular equivalent of the Second Coming.

One newspaper article explained that atomic power would soon drive cars, abolish coal mining and release humanity from drudgery. Cheap energy would desalinate the oceans, irrigate the deserts and create an “earthly paradise”. The atom, having incinerated Hiroshima and Nagasaki, was to become mankind’s fairy godmother. Its radiation would preserve food, cure disease and perhaps persuade the washing-up to do itself.

Eisenhower promised to beat the nuclear sword into a ploughshare. Fissionable materials, instead of being concentrated in the arsenals of the great powers, would be distributed to the “power-starved areas of the world”. Access to this miraculous technology would provide the incentive for poorer nations to forswear nuclear weapons. The bomb would be dismantled and reborn as a turbine.

The International Atomic Energy Agency was created to implement this vision. Its statute requires it both to ensure that nuclear assistance is not used for military purposes and to “accelerate and enlarge” the contribution of atomic energy to peace and prosperity. The nuclear non-proliferation treaty repeated the bargain. States without bombs promised not to acquire them. In return they received an “inalienable right” to develop nuclear energy and to obtain the materials, knowledge and equipment required to do so. The five recognised nuclear powers promised to disarm.

The programme made sense only while the dream was still dreamable. Once it became clear that a civilian nuclear industry provides almost everything a government requires to build a bomb, Atoms for Peace should have been renamed Bombs for Anyone.

Every state which developed nuclear weapons over the past 30 years did so by diverting resources or expertise from a civilian programme: Israel, South Africa, India, Pakistan, North Korea and Iraq. In several cases the technologies were supplied directly under Atoms for Peace. The reactors, fuel cycles, laboratories and trained physicists were delivered in the name of preventing the outcome they made possible.

India’s first bomb was described as a “peaceful nuclear explosion”, which is rather like describing a decapitation as radical physiotherapy. Its programme was assisted at various times by Canada, the United States, Germany, France, Norway and the United Kingdom. Canada supplied the reactor; the US supplied heavy water; European states provided equipment, fuel and expertise. Pakistan, watching its enemy being fitted out for Armageddon by the peace-loving powers, demanded parallel assistance and received much of it. The rich world was equipping both sides for total war, while congratulating itself on spreading enlightenment.

Around 20 countries are now believed to possess the technical capacity to become nuclear weapons states within months. Most acquired it through foreign assistance to their civilian programmes. The non-proliferation system has not prevented them from approaching the threshold. It has escorted them there.

The absurdity reaches its purest form in Iran. Russia was not merely permitted to supply uranium and nuclear technology to the Iranian government. Under the treaty’s commitment to peaceful cooperation, it was obliged to assist. The equipment used to generate electricity and the equipment used to prepare for nuclear war are, until the final stages, much the same equipment.

Iran is enriching uranium and producing heavy water. Both activities are legitimate components of civilian nuclear programmes. Both can also be used to make bombs. Uranium enriched to the level required by a power reactor is not yet weapons-grade, but the most difficult work has already been done. The remaining enrichment requires further passes through the centrifuges. Heavy water permits the operation of reactors capable of producing plutonium. The distance between peaceful use and military use is not a wall. It is a corridor.

So the treaty gives Iran both the means and the alibi. If you want a bomb, the sensible course is not to remain outside the international system. You sign the treaties, join the agency, demand your entitlements, import the technology, train your scientists and insist that every suspicious activity is peaceful. Once the difficult infrastructure has been assembled, you can withdraw from the treaty, obstruct inspectors or simply argue about definitions until the warheads are ready.

Iran also has powerful reasons to seek them. Israel possesses a large nuclear arsenal, refuses to sign the non-proliferation treaty and is protected from serious pressure by the United States. The US and the UK, while lecturing Iran about its obligations, have abandoned the spirit of their own promise to disarm. Both have contemplated the pre-emptive use of nuclear weapons. American forces and bases surround Iran: in Iraq, Afghanistan, Turkey, the Gulf and central Asia.

Then there is the instructive case of North Korea. If you lack weapons of mass destruction, the United States might invade you to find them. If you are building them, it threatens to invade. If you possess them, it opens negotiations. Nuclear weapons confer not only destructive power but diplomatic immunity. If Iran is not attempting to acquire them, it has grievously misunderstood the conduct of world politics.

What, then, can El Baradei do? He can plead. He can request transparency. He can urge Iran to accept “voluntary agreements”. But the treaty he is supposed to enforce does not grant him the power to prohibit enrichment undertaken under safeguards. When an activity is legal, the inspector must beg the inspected state to stop it.

The official nuclear powers have meanwhile junked their side of the bargain. They modernise their arsenals, develop new uses for them and insist that their security requires what everyone else’s security must reject. The non-nuclear states exploit the treaty’s technological promises, while the nuclear states ignore its disarmament promises. It is a system in which everyone cheats honestly.

The possible consequences are almost too horrible to contemplate. An Iranian bomb could precipitate a nuclear cascade across the Middle East. Saudi Arabia, Egypt and Turkey would be unlikely to sit still. A Hezbollah attack on Israel could provoke retaliation against Iran; an Israeli strike on Iranian facilities could trigger a regional war. The first nuclear arms race recruited a handful of governments. The second could recruit almost everyone.

There is one conclusion which respectable opinion refuses to articulate. You cannot phase out nuclear weapons without phasing out nuclear power. A technology which generates fissile materials, trains nuclear engineers and normalises the possession of enrichment and reprocessing plants cannot be permanently separated from the bomb. Inspections can delay proliferation. They cannot abolish the knowledge or equipment on which it depends.

We need a new nuclear treaty based not on Eisenhower’s utopian fantasy but on the grim facts his successors discovered. It must require the verified elimination of both arsenals and the civilian systems which sustain them, while providing genuine alternatives for energy and development. Otherwise the IAEA will continue to manufacture the danger it is supposed to contain. Its director will remain the man required to destroy the world in order to save it.

author: George Monbiot
title: cross_topic_1/test/1/test_106
body:
Why does a country full of brilliant people so often end up choosing between two unimpressive men? The answer is blunt: because unimpressive men keep getting elected. Every four years, the political machine presents its latest products, and every four years those who dislike the machine are instructed to endorse one of them. This year the progressive establishment is once again telling voters to choose the safer, duller option.

Its argument deserves to be heard. Nearly every major progressive voice—the Guardian, the Nation magazine, even Noam Chomsky—has urged voters in the swing states to support John Kerry. Kerry might be about as inspiring as a parking lot on a wet Sunday in Detroit, but he is the only candidate capable of defeating George Bush. The contest is so close that a few thousand votes for Ralph Nader in Florida, Pennsylvania or Ohio might return the president to office. Some Republicans, understanding the calculation perfectly, have funnelled money towards Nader’s campaign. If your enemies are helping your preferred candidate, the Democrats point out, you might reasonably wonder whose interests he serves.

This case has real merit. Bush has launched two unnecessary wars and threatened dozens of nations with the possibility of attack. He has shredded international treaties and domestic regulations, granted corporations licence to accelerate climate change, globalised the assault on civil liberties and begun dismantling the idea that the state should tax the rich and help the poor. His government has converted private greed into public doctrine and national paranoia into foreign policy. The world would plainly be safer without him.

These, we are told, are exceptional circumstances. A Guardian leader argues that whatever Kerry’s flaws, they are overshadowed by Bush’s neoconservative agenda and catastrophic war-making. The Nation, in an open letter to Ralph Nader, insists that “2004 is not 2000” and that “the defeat of George W Bush is critical”. The implication is that the normal business of building a progressive alternative must be suspended until the emergency has passed.

There is just one difficulty. The emergency never passes.

Both publications made much the same case in 2000. After Al Gore’s defeat, the Nation’s Eric Alterman attributed the result to Nader’s “megalomania”. Before the election, the Guardian described Gore as the “safe, wise choice”, while conceding that he was neither exciting nor visionary. Then, as now, the progressive candidate was accused of endangering civilisation by refusing to withdraw. Then, as now, the Democrat’s defects were said to be regrettable but temporarily irrelevant.

Look back through almost any modern presidential election and you will find the same warning. The Republican is uniquely terrible; the Democrat is uniquely capable of stopping him; the election is uniquely close; reform must be postponed for four more years. The language changes, while the trap remains.

The American electoral system, built from patronage, corruption and media fear, will reliably produce dreadful candidates. The corporations buy the advertising; the television networks determine which opinions are respectable; the parties exclude competitors; the donors choose the plausible candidates before the voters see a ballot. A system of this kind will always manufacture exceptional circumstances. Only a leader prepared to overturn it and reintroduce democracy can end the cycle. Voting for the bad to stop the terrible in 2004 ensures that the same choice will be presented in 2008, then in 2012, and onward until the republic expires from caution.

Changing this system requires political courage. It means confronting the corporations that purchase elections and the media companies that police the boundaries of debate. Kerry displayed genuine courage in Vietnam. On the campaign trail he has displayed none.

Asked about his vote authorising the invasion of Iraq, he replied that “even knowing what we now know” he would still have voted for it. His national security adviser, James Rubin, told the Washington Post that Kerry would “in all probability” have attacked Iraq by now had he been president. The challenger’s supposed distinction from Bush therefore consists partly in claiming that he might have prosecuted the same disaster more competently.

His supporters boast of his fundraising. But money raised from corporate executives is not evidence of democratic enthusiasm. It is investment. The people financing Kerry expect access, restraint and obedience. They are unlikely to have misjudged him. When Kerry gets his orders, he reports for duty.

Can anyone seriously imagine this frightened, flinching candidate supervising a democratic revolution? He has made the system work for him by working for the system. He knows that if he challenged the interests sustaining his campaign, they would destroy him. Unlike Hugo Chávez, he possesses neither the charisma nor the popular enthusiasm with which a leader might confront entrenched wealth. He has donors, consultants, endorsements and a splendid résumé. What he lacks is a movement.

A vote for Kerry, then, is not merely a vote against Bush. It is a vote for the survival of the system that made Bush possible.

This is why Ralph Nader matters. I disagree with some of his positions—on trade, for example—but no one can plausibly deny his courage. His decision to stand despite near-universal opposition from the progressive establishment is both brave and foolhardy. For decades he has fought corporations whose resources dwarf his own and endured the predictable attacks of a media that treats resistance to corporate power as a personality disorder.

He has even been willing to confront the Anti-Defamation League, which he accuses of smearing critics of Israeli policy as antisemites. Whatever judgment you make about this dispute, it is hard to identify another American politician prepared to enter it. Most of them sprint for cover as soon as the subject is raised.

Nader will not win. But this observation settles less than his opponents suppose. The purpose of his campaign is to establish that systemic change must begin now, rather than in the radiant future to which it is endlessly deferred. The better he performs, the sooner the movement for reform can grow into a force the major parties cannot ignore.

The Nation suggests that he would exert more influence as a “public citizen”, demanding open presidential debates and rallying voters behind progressive Democrats. But what incentive would the Democrats have to listen? If Nader promises never to threaten their vote, they can safely ignore every demand he makes. He holds leverage over these cowering creatures only while they fear he will take their supporters away.

There are, of course, differences between Kerry and Bush. Bush is active; Kerry is passive. Kerry wants high defence spending, but would probably be more reluctant to attack other nations. He wants tax cuts, but is less willing to eviscerate health and education. He wants lower oil prices, but will not help the corporations tear open the Arctic Wildlife Refuge. These distinctions are not imaginary. For people exposed to the immediate consequences of government policy, some could be important.

But are they sufficient to justify abandoning the only current electoral effort to democratise American politics? No. If progressives always surrender their programme whenever the Republicans nominate a dangerous man, they surrender it for ever. The party most practised in manufacturing dangerous men thereby acquires a permanent veto over political change.

The progressive establishment is asking voters to trade liberty for security and democracy for expediency. It promises that this retreat is temporary, though it has made the same promise before and will make it again. Its strategy prevents catastrophe today by preserving the machinery that manufactures catastrophe tomorrow.

The voters should ignore them.

author: George Monbiot
title: cross_topic_1/validation/1/validation_5
body:
Nick Raynsford, the planning minister, has been accused of concealing a decision to permit a listed building in Oxford to be moved, clearing the way for a business school funded by the arms broker Wafic Said. Mr Raynsford denies intervening. His defence is formidable: when the decision was taken, he was in Egypt, leading a trade mission. Unless the minister has mastered bilocation, this appears to dispose of the charge.

But it raises a more interesting question. What was the minister responsible for regulating development doing leading a commercial delegation to Egypt? His alibi, in other words, may reveal a graver problem than the alleged offence.

Mr Raynsford travelled with representatives of 27 corporations. They included Balfour Beatty, Enron, Laing, Mott MacDonald, Thames Water and WS Atkins. Their itinerary embraced the Toshka project, the Egyptian government’s attempt to divert water from the Nile and transform the western desert. It is expected to cost around $90bn. Environmentalists have warned that it could damage the river and accelerate the salinisation of farmland. Financial consultants have questioned whether its extravagant promises bear any relation to economic reality.

This is precisely the kind of project which, were it proposed in Britain, would fall under the scrutiny of the planning minister. In Egypt, however, he was not inspecting it on behalf of the public. He was helping corporations to secure a share of it. The man charged at home with asking whether development should proceed was abroad asking whether British companies could build it.
