Even here, the story grows stranger. The principal contract was not won by a British corporation, but by Kvaerner, the Norwegian engineering company. Why should a British minister’s mission have assisted a foreign firm?

One possible explanation lies inside his own department. Kvaerner had seconded more employees to the Department of the Environment than any other company. The regulator, it seems, was being staffed in part by the businesses whose interests it was supposed to regulate. When corporate representatives pass through government departments as freely as commuters through a railway station, it becomes difficult to know where public administration ends and commercial promotion begins.

Mr Raynsford’s predicament is not accidental. It is written into his job description. He is both minister for planning and minister for construction: simultaneously the referee and the team manager. As planning minister, he should protect the public from destructive development. As construction minister, he promotes the industry seeking permission to develop. The conflict is permanent, but its resolution appears remarkably consistent.

He explained his priorities to Building magazine. His task, he said, was “creating a climate in which the industry can do well”. This is an intelligible ambition for a construction lobbyist. It is a peculiar declaration from the minister responsible for deciding where, when and whether construction should happen.

His record suggests that the phrase was not carelessly chosen. He sponsored Minerals 98, a public relations campaign enabling quarrying companies to lobby against the government’s proposed tax on roadstone. At Labour’s 1998 local government conference, he argued against taxing parking spaces at out-of-town developments. He promised the British Council of Shopping Centres that he would accelerate retail schemes. He weakened proposed energy-efficiency rules for new homes because, he maintained, they would place “an undue burden on builders”.

Each intervention can doubtless be defended in isolation. Taken together, they describe a ministry whose regulatory functions have been subordinated to the convenience of the regulated.

The most serious allegation concerns the Ilisu dam in Turkey. The project would flood Kurdish settlements and displace some 78,000 people. Human rights campaigners argue that, in a region where Kurdish villages have already been destroyed and their inhabitants expelled, this amounts to engineering ethnic cleansing.

In September 1999, Mr Raynsford led another trade mission, this time to Turkey. He accompanied a consortium fronted by Balfour Beatty, which hoped to secure £4bn of contracts for reconstruction after the earthquake. Balfour Beatty also wanted to build the Ilisu dam. Three months later, the British government announced that it was “minded to grant” export credit for the project.

It has been alleged that Mr Raynsford persuaded Tony Blair to support the dam. If so, an obvious question follows. Was British assistance for Ilisu connected to the contracts being sought by the consortium? Were the commercial opportunities discussed in Turkey made conditional, formally or otherwise, upon government backing for the dam? Until the correspondence and minutes are published, we cannot know. But the sequence is sufficiently disturbing to demand an answer.

Which returns us to Oxford. Everything Mr Raynsford says about the business school may be true. There may have been no concealment, no improper intervention and nothing unusual about the decision to move the listed building.

That is the scandal. There was nothing unusual about it.

Turning a blind eye to the destruction of landscapes, communities and historic places on behalf of the construction industry is not an aberration requiring a ministerial cover-up. It is routine government business, conducted by the man supposedly appointed to prevent it. The problem is not that Nick Raynsford might once have failed to police the developers. It is that the government has made their promoter their policeman.

author: Hugo Young
title: cross_topic_1/test/2/test_119
body:
I have been coming to America for more than 30 years. I studied here, reported elections here, travelled through states that many Americans never visit, accumulated friends and connections, and finally married an American. I thought I knew the place. America was not foreign in the ordinary sense. Its habits, boasts, anxieties and incomparable simplicities had become familiar material.

But I did not know it. This is the discovery of the post-September world. Perhaps America did not know itself either. It took the destruction of September 11, followed by the Afghan war, to force upon this famously forward-looking country an examination of what it was. The result is not that America has changed. Nothing fundamental has changed. Everything is more so.

I spent Christmas in a remote corner of Vermont, which is an audacious place from which to generalise about 280m people. This is a liberal, rural, almost tourist-free part of the most liberal state in the union. It cannot stand for Texas or California, still less New York. Yet national character has a way of declaring itself most clearly at the edges. Here, among snow, woodsmoke and people whose politics are instinctively suspicious of Washington, America after September was visible.

Consider consideration. Americans were always considerate people. They ask after health and family with a directness Europeans sometimes mistake for superficiality. They help strangers. They remember birthdays. Now they are super-caring. The attack was collectively shared, not confined to the bereaved or to New Yorkers. Three months on, the shock still reverberates through conversations that start somewhere else and find their way back to the towers.

There has been no stoical sloughing-off. Americans have not decided that enough mourning has happened and normal life must now resume. Normal life has resumed, but carries the wound inside it. People blunder about, searching for meaning, sometimes finding only cliches. Their answer is to care more tenderly. Every departure matters. Every aeroplane journey is accompanied by calls at either end. The private emotions of friendship and family have become part of the national defence.

Second, self-confidence. There is no whining. There is remarkably little self-pity. The country does not ask why the world failed to protect it. Self-sufficiency, material and moral, is second nature. The certainties that must have maddened Osama bin Laden before September have redoubled since. America was attacked for being America. The response is to become more emphatically itself.

Humour has returned too, after a period when solemnity seemed compulsory. Garrison Keillor, on his radio show, now makes jihad jokes. The audience laughs without the nervous preliminary calculation that once attended any reference to Islam. This may be unjust to millions of Muslims, and sometimes is. But Islam has been dropped from political correctness. The change is not evidence of recovered frivolity so much as confidence restored. Americans have decided they are allowed to laugh at what tried to kill them.

Third, patriotism. Flags always hung in Vermont yards. This is not a part of America that needed September 11 to discover the stars and stripes. But the intensity is different. Flags are everywhere: on barns, bridges, filling stations, cars, school walls. They are not argumentative flags. They make no case. They state a condition of belonging.

The Vermont irony is considerable. This state sends to Congress a man who describes himself as a socialist. It has little affection for George W Bush, and less for the hard-right interests around him. Yet the patriotic response to September is unquestioning. Bush may be distrusted as a Republican politician. He is supported as commander-in-chief.

Even the local newspapers disclose the change. The Valley News of White River Junction serves an area where news is normally very local indeed. Yet it leads with the swearing-in of Hamid Karzai, and carries news of Yasser Arafat's visit to Bethlehem. This is not a sudden internationalism. Vermonters have not become students of the tribal composition of Afghanistan, or the clauses of the Oslo agreement. What has disappeared is indifference towards the world from which the threat comes.

America's role in that world is still conceived with crystalline simplicity. It is to protect America. Other good things may follow: freedom for Afghans, food for refugees, possibly a better government. But these are ancillary. The purpose is American safety, and there is nothing shameful about that.

Fourth, constitutionalism. Here the change is more marked, and more troubling. Liberal acquaintances who would once have reacted fiercely to any curtailment of due process now raise only modest objections to military tribunals, secret evidence and indefinite detention. They do not necessarily approve. But approval has ceased to be the test. Necessity is enough.

The government cites Lincoln and Franklin Roosevelt. Habeas corpus was suspended before. Free speech was curtailed in other times of threat. This seems to satisfy people, though precedent is not the same as justification. A country whose constitution is almost a civil religion has become more constitutionalist in rhetoric, while becoming less exacting about constitutional liberty in practice. The flag protects the constitution. For the moment, it also protects those who bend it.

Domestic politics, meanwhile, continues. Budget arguments proceed. Left and right dispute taxation. Politicians manoeuvre for the 2002 elections. On these matters America is as noisy and divided as ever. But foreign politics has virtually ceased. The country has become easy to lead. Alarmingly so.

Even people who distrust Bush trust him because there appears to be no alternative. He will “get the job done”. This is the archetypal American phrase, managerial, optimistic, innocent of complication. What job, across how many countries, at what cost and for how long? These are secondary questions. The job exists. America will do it.

At a Christmas gathering, friends joked about the possibility of being “blown to pieces”. The words were casual, almost cheerful. Beneath them lay a new fatalism. Fate has taken over. America must do what America must do, which is to extinguish al-Qaida wherever it survives.

American idealism has often been fickle in the diplomacy that follows war. Now it has a purpose on which everyone agrees: saving America itself. What this means for the world is obscure. What the world means for America is clearer than it has been since Jefferson: safety from sea to shining sea, and maybe not beyond.

author: Hugo Young
title: cross_topic_1/test/2/test_13
body:
Law and order politics is entirely about spin. Not partly, or even mainly. Entirely. Some of the impressions happen to be correct. Crime has roughly doubled in 20 years. The public genuinely wants heavier sentencing, especially for crimes involving violence. But these truths scarcely alter the character of the trade. The raw material politicians handle is propaganda, not fact. In no other area of political conflict does impression so completely eclipse reality, or the facts lend themselves so obediently to the manufacture of mutually contradictory certainties.

David Blunkett says Labour has produced the best crime record for 30 years. Oliver Letwin says the Conservative record was the best for 50. Both claims, within limits, are defensible. Everything depends on the year from which counting starts, the offences counted, the population adjustment, the police force selected, and whether recorded crime or the British Crime Survey is being quoted. It depends, in other words, on which truth the speaker needs.

The voter cannot possibly arbitrate. Nor can most journalists, confronted at four o'clock with 70 pages of Home Office figures and required to pronounce by six. The statistics are intrinsically complicated, then politically shaded. A rise may prove confidence in the police has improved, causing more victims to report. A fall may prove policing has improved. Either way, the minister wins. For the opposition, naturally, either way he loses.

Consider the audit of Labour's record conducted by Nicholas Timmins and David Walker in Labour's First Term, published by Politico's. Its crime chapter gives no comfort to absolutists. Domestic burglary fell by about 20%. Car theft fell by 15%. Violence, measured by the survey rather than police records, declined modestly. But street robbery rose sharply. Clear-up rates scarcely moved, while convictions increased. This is a mixed picture because reality is mixed. It contains progress, failure and several outcomes whose meaning remains uncertain.

Mr Blunkett cannot put that on a poster. Mr Letwin would be foolish to try. Each therefore extracts the fragment that proves his pre-existing proposition. Sophistication, however true, will always be outgunned by simplicity, however false.

The public brings its own contribution to this corruption. The latest national crime survey shows overwhelming belief that judges are too lenient and out of touch. This is presented as a fact judges must respect. Yet the same survey reveals startling ignorance about what judges actually do. More than half the respondents underestimate average sentences by at least 30%. They believe only half of convicted rapists go to prison. The real figure is 97%.

The misconceptions extend to burglary and mugging. People routinely imagine sentences far below those imposed. They suppose repeat burglars walk away with fines, and violent street thieves receive community service. These grotesque misperceptions are the foundation of the judiciary's reputation as a soft touch.

The image has been brainlessly cultivated by the tabloid press, for which the atypical sentence is always the representative one. One supposedly indulgent judge supplies more usable indignation than 10,000 routine custodial decisions. The poison then seeps upwards. Ministers cite public alarm, much of it created by newspapers; newspapers cite ministerial concern as proof that the alarm is justified. Policy emerges from a closed circuit of mutually manufactured outrage.

This is not new. The previous Conservative government passed a criminal justice act virtually every year, until later statutes began undoing what earlier ones had done. Michael Howard perfected legislation as a form of declamation. Each act was advertised as the decisive restoration of order. If it had been decisive, the next one would not have been necessary.

Labour's record is less simple than either side admits. Curfew orders and anti-social behaviour orders were reasonable in design. In practice, too few have been used, too many breached, and enforcement has been erratic. Their initials, ASBO, have travelled further than their effects. Here was policy almost born as a press release.

The latest reform package is not irrational. It allows judges to give greater weight to previous convictions when sentencing. This addresses the persuasive analysis that a relatively small population, roughly 100,000 persistent offenders, commits a very large share of all crime. Habit should matter. A fifteenth burglary is not morally or socially identical to a first.

It will spin well. Persistent criminals will be locked away. The opposition will spin against it just as easily. Too little, too late; another surrender to the criminals; another promise without prison places. Between these rival narratives, three conclusions stand out.

First, the best policies cost money. The present package is priced at £1.2bn over three years. This sounds substantial until divided among new custodial regimes, accelerated court hearings, probation programmes and increased police manpower. The single most persuasive remedy across the confusing statistics is a larger, more visible, better managed police force.

Here Mr Blunkett's failure is conspicuous. Recruitment promises have not been met. Some forces are growing, others merely replacing departures. The national total remains below the figure promised with such precision. Is the Treasury committed to sustained expansion, including salaries, training and pensions? Or is £1.2bn another propaganda sum, timed to mature before the local elections on May 1 2003?

Second, there is a hole at the heart of the crime agenda. Ministers endlessly offer drugs as the explanation for crime, but refuse to address drug laws as a focus for reform. Police chiefs, doctors, magistrates and prison governors press them to think. The spinners decline.

Decriminalising cannabis would eliminate a category of crime at a stroke. It would release police time, court time and prison resources. It might permit a clearer concentration on heroin and crack, which drive acquisitive crime and destroy lives. There are arguments against reform. But they are not being heard because the very discussion is judged electorally toxic. The spinners dare not look at it.

Third, prisons keep multiplying. Britain has the highest imprisonment rate in Europe. England and Wales also stand joint first among industrialised nations for crime as experienced by victims. No accountable politician will connect these two facts. More imprisonment and more victimisation coexist, but the electoral auction proceeds on the assumption that another thousand cells must represent another thousand victories.

Recent critiques have been clinical as well as passionate. Lord Woolf, the lord chief justice, has warned against the promiscuous use of custody. Sir David Ramsbotham, the former chief inspector of prisons, has described institutions reduced to warehouses of idleness and despair. Martin Narey, director general of the prison service, has spoken with unusual candour about overcrowding, drugs and the near-impossibility of rehabilitation under present conditions.

This is not a chorus of sentimentalists. These men have seen the system from the inside. Their conclusion is that prison, indispensable for dangerous offenders, is being made less effective by indiscriminate expansion. But the electoral auctioneers dare not change the terms of debate. Any suggestion that fewer people should be jailed is translated, before the sentence ends, into softness on crime.

Spinners can appeal only to the lowest denominator of opinion, however ignorant. Their task is not to improve understanding but to occupy its prejudices. The spinner always, more or less, lies: sometimes through the statistic selected, sometimes through the context omitted, sometimes through the promise whose financing is kept deliberately obscure.

This does not mean crime is imaginary, or fear foolish. Both are real. It means the politics surrounding them has become peculiarly incapable of seriousness. Nobody can any longer sort one spinner from another. The beginning of wisdom in law and order would be for someone to start the arduous business of addressing the truth.

author: Hugo Young
title: cross_topic_1/test/2/test_16
body:
It is hard to believe the Northern Ireland peace process could fail now. Not merely regrettable, or tragic, or another turn of the historical wheel, but incredible. So much distance has been travelled, so many certainties dismantled, so much blood-soaked rhetoric consigned to the archive, that collapse at this point would look like a feat of deliberate perversity. Yet the end game was always going to be the hardest part. The conflict is no longer principally about power, territory or religion. It is about attitudes towards mutual surrender: who admits first that the old struggle has gone, and what physical evidence must be produced to validate the admission.

This is why the crisis may defeat them. Two forms of real sincerity are ranged against each other. Each side has made concessions which, considered against its own history, are formidable. Each believes the other is now demanding the one concession that cannot honourably be made. Each sees itself as having crossed the larger distance. In the narrow space between those convictions lies the possibility that the Good Friday agreement, having survived everything substantial, will perish over the meaning of a symbol.

Start with the republicans. On their side there is a sincere belief that the war is over. Sinn Fein-IRA, under Gerry Adams and Martin McGuinness, has shifted on to a political track with a completeness that would have been unimaginable not many years ago. The IRA now declares its “total” commitment to a “permanent peace”. These words are not nothing. They come from an organisation whose entire purpose, discipline and mythology were built around the proposition that armed struggle was not only legitimate but indispensable.

The Adams argument has great force. Republicanism has, in practice, abandoned the constitutional claim that its violence once purported to enforce. Sinn Fein is preparing to run the country it used to attack. Its ministers sit in government with unionists. Its leaders talk to the British government not as intermediaries for a war, but as politicians negotiating the terms of peace. Consent has replaced coercion as the accepted mechanism by which the constitutional position of Northern Ireland might eventually change.

This U-turn is credible partly because republicans resisted it for so long. Nobody can suppose that Adams and McGuinness arrived here casually, or without danger from people behind them. Every adjustment has required them to reinterpret the lives and deaths of comrades who were told that military victory remained possible. They have had to explain why prisoners could be released into a world where the objective for which they fought is postponed indefinitely, perhaps for ever. The scale of that internal revolution should not be belittled.

From this perspective, decommissioning is achievable but beside the point. The guns are silent. The command structure has accepted politics. General de Chastelaine may inspect, certify and report, but what matters is the de facto extinction of violence, not the theatrical disposal of a few weapons from an arsenal that nobody imagines will be wholly destroyed. If peace is permanent, the arms are already redundant. A promise sustained in practice is more significant, republicans say, than a performance arranged to satisfy the tribal anxieties of David Trimble.

Now consider the unionists. Their sincerity is no smaller. They too have accepted what once seemed unthinkable. They have admitted republicans into power and started running a government with them. Men whose associates were connected with murder have been treated as colleagues in an executive created under the Good Friday agreement. This is not a technical concession. It has required the overcoming of vast obstacles in the unionist psyche: memories of funerals, inherited mistrust of Dublin, fear that every compromise is merely another stage in Britain’s departure.

Trimble has faced supporters whose hatred is not disguised, and opponents who make a career out of calling every act of statesmanship betrayal. He has also had to contend with parts of the London press whose interest in Ulster becomes most animated when failure can be predicted, preferably with treachery attached to his name. Against this background, his manoeuvring past the accumulated negatives of unionist history deserves to be called statesmanship. He has not led his party with perfect grace. Nobody could. But he has led it somewhere it had sworn never to go.

Unionists have already lived with procrastination over decommissioning. Deadlines have come and gone. Formulas have been manufactured, clarified and diluted. The institutions were established without the prior surrender of arms that many unionists believed the agreement implied. They are also being asked to swallow most of the Patten report on the RUC, which touches another nerve of identity and sacrifice. The police service that stood between their community and terrorism is to be transformed in name, composition and symbolism. Unionists may object noisily, but most of Patten will be endured.

To outsiders, especially those not steeped in the locked psychology of Ulster politics, the answer seems obvious. Unionists should take one more stride. If decommissioning has not happened by the May 22 deadline, they should nevertheless keep the executive alive. Having swallowed the camel, are they really straining at the gnat? Having accepted Sinn Fein ministers, prisoner releases, constitutional change and police reform, can they rationally insist that peace depends on the transfer of a token quantity of guns to General de Chastelaine?

This view has become almost a mainland orthodoxy. Trimble’s threatened resignation if decommissioning is not delivered is treated as a self-executing catastrophe. If he goes, the executive may fall. If the executive falls, violence may revive. Therefore, it is said, the blood would be on unionist hands. They will have chosen symbolism over peace, party survival over historic duty, one last negative over the positive architecture already built.

The argument has power. But it also carries the familiar condescension towards unionists: the assumption that only they are required to make concessions in the real world, while republicans must forever be protected from the emotional consequences of theirs. It is apparently realistic to tell Trimble to defy his party, but insensitive to ask Adams and McGuinness to use their influence. Unionist history must be transcended. Republican history must be understood.

There comes a point when understanding becomes indulgence. It is said to be insensitive, even dangerous, to talk about blame. But responsibility cannot be abolished merely because the hour is critical. In this crisis, it is the republicans who are on trial. Realpolitik has reached its limit. The practical case for accepting the permanence of IRA weapons has been conceded. Nobody imagines full disarmament. Nobody expects the military capability to disappear by May 22. What is required is not a strategic capitulation, but a decisive—though entirely incomplete—surrender.

A token handover would be substantively meaningless. A few guns put beyond use would not disable the IRA. They would not prevent some future faction from finding other weapons. In military terms, the act would be close to a nullity. But politics is made partly of such nullities. Symbols become indispensable when they establish that words have crossed into conduct.

The IRA says the war is over. It says its peace is permanent. These assurances may be sincere. But in the real world a promise is not enough, especially when it comes from an army that preserved secrecy, discipline and lethal capacity as the instruments of its existence. The unbearable discomfort of a necessary gesture is precisely what gives the gesture value. It would prove that republicanism has entered the world it chose to belong to: a world where trust is not demanded as tribute, but earned by visible acts.

Adams and McGuinness have brought their movement an immense distance. They now have to show that the journey was not conditional on everybody else suspending disbelief. The guns need not all go. Some must. Everything else is bunker piety.

author: Hugo Young
title: cross_topic_1/validation/2/validation_11
body:
Every party now agrees that ideology is dead. The word is as dirty to Conservatives selecting a leader as it is to a centre-left prime minister addressing the future of public services. Who regrets its passing? Not many politicians, certainly. But there is a cost. Without ideology, politics becomes more necessary to conduct and harder to explain. The old silver thread has snapped. When the left said public good, private bad, and the right offered approximately the reverse, almost anyone could follow the argument. Government might fail, but at least one knew what it thought it was doing. Now the doing is more complicated, and the understanding harder still.

Political practice has had no alternative but to recognise this. Public understanding has not caught up. It continues to reach for the old simplicities, applying ideological labels to arrangements devised after ideology. The mindset survives the system that created it. Newspaper headlines naturally search for a familiar matrix, but they are not alone. Most people, I suspect, hear that private companies may help deliver operations or rescue schools and conclude that the government intends a private-sector takeover. Reform is translated into privatisation before anyone has examined what is proposed.

For this confusion the government bears much responsibility. During its first term it was not straight. Feeling its way, and terrified of alarming anyone, it tried to please everyone at once. Business was reassured with talk of competition, innovation and enterprise. Labour's own constituency was soothed with assurances that nothing fundamental would change. Ministers could sound tough in the morning and tender by teatime. The health service was to be transformed, but also preserved exactly as it was. Schools were to face new disciplines, while every existing interest was told it had nothing to fear.

The substitute for ideology was the famous doctrine of “what works”. This sounded admirably empirical. Unfortunately, it became the governing philosophy before the government knew what worked. Ministers understood that much of what the Conservatives had attempted, especially in the NHS, had failed. They were less certain what should replace it. “What works” therefore became less a conclusion than an alibi: an open-ended permission to experiment, retreat, rename and try again.

Some confusion remains inevitable. Post-ideological government is intrinsically untidy. But important distinctions must still be made. Enlisting private-sector skills and capital to establish surgical centres, or bringing outside management into a failing school, is not the same as privatisation. A service can remain publicly financed, publicly guaranteed and publicly accountable while some of its work is performed by people who are not public employees.

Very little of the public sector could in any event be run by the private sector. The fantasy of a wholesale commercial takeover belongs equally to zealots who desire it and unions that fear it. Hospitals, schools and the innumerable obligations attached to them cannot simply be sold into a market and left there. Instituting competition, or the uglier word “contestability”, does not abolish public-service principles. Across France, Germany, the Netherlands and other continental systems, it is routine. It is not treated as an American assault on social solidarity, but as one instrument for lifting performance towards standards citizens are entitled to expect.

The prime minister’s major speech has now clarified more of this than any previous intervention. It was the most important address he has made since re-election. The headline will be his promise not to “flinch” from opposition by the producer unions. That is the easy dramatic line. Some union leaders will hear in it a desire for combat, perhaps even a search for his own version of Margaret Thatcher’s collision with the miners.

But the speech was not Thatcherite in either sound or purpose. Mrs Thatcher relished the naming of enemies. Her language advanced in armour. This was a sober, not a militant, performance. It sounded less like a declaration from a conqueror than a message from the last chance saloon of British social democracy. A government that believes in public provision has rather late awakened to the danger that the public may conclude public provision does not work.

That danger is real. Most users of schools and hospitals know that more money is essential. They also know, from experience, that money is not sufficient. Delivery depends on organisation, incentives, management, professional commitment and the awkward detail of making institutions respond. The speech accepted this reality without pretending that one slogan could master it. In doing so, it sent three signals.

First, there is no Great Blueprint. Different failures require different remedies. If inspection reports describe more than half of local education authorities as fairly or very successful, abolishing all of them would be doctrinaire nonsense. Successful authorities should continue. Failed ones must change. Equally, it makes no sense to insist that only existing NHS units may tender to run new surgical centres merely because those centres supplement NHS hospitals. The question is what arrangement gets patients treated quickly, safely and without charge, not what badge appears on every employee’s payslip.

This variety will disappoint both ideological camps. It is meant to. Government cannot reform institutions by endlessly redrawing organisational charts, though ministers often prefer that activity to the tedious practicalities. Nor can it declare war on every union and call the resulting noise progress. The prime minister’s deeper point was simpler. In a country at peace, with high employment and unprecedented resources, no governmental task is more pressing than making the basic materials of a civilised society work properly.

Second, only a social-democratic government has a plausible chance of carrying this through. Only a party whose belief in the primacy of public services is beyond serious dispute can be trusted to alter their delivery without allowing reform to become merely a device for enriching business and depriving the disadvantaged. That trust is not limitless. It has to be earned. But the historic credentials matter.

The Conservatives have noticed this. Their leadership contest is full of candidates professing attachment to health and education. Most now admit that their party’s failure in these fields contributed powerfully to its rejection. Two speak as though public services have become central to modern Conservatism. But another embodies the hard right’s recurrent flirtation with the minimal state, private insurance and the American model. That candidate may yet win. The persistence of this instinct reveals what is at stake. Unless progressive politicians produce visible improvement, the public realm will breed deeper cynicism, and eventually create the conditions for its own abandonment.

Third, the challenge may be impossible fully to meet. The prime minister has staked his project on outcomes that will never look satisfactory to everyone. Expectations rise with expenditure. Every improvement creates a demand for the next one. The famous glass in the classroom or hospital ward is always liable to appear half empty. National statistics showing shorter waits will coexist with the patient who waited intolerably. Better examination results will not silence the parent whose child was failed. Politics cannot conclusively win an argument composed of millions of personal experiences.

There is also the economy. Investment rests on continuing prosperity and the budget surplus prudence has secured. A prolonged global downturn, dragging Britain after it, could destroy the arithmetic. Not even the chancellor’s caution can guarantee the resources on which the programme depends. Social democracy may discover that its defining promise is hostage to forces beyond its control.

All that can safely be said is that the prime minister has now begun the defining stage of his project. He has done so without glamour, and for once without much spin. He offers no ideological comfort, no universal mechanism, no painless guarantee of success. He has merely recognised the reality people most value, and the danger his government most needs to overcome. Even that is worth recognising. More than that, for the moment, it is worth supporting.

author: Hugo Young
title: cross_topic_1/validation/2/validation_12
body:
Between the first Labour term and the second, before the new mandate wipes clean the memory of the old, there is a moment for taking stock. Elections are designed to confer legitimacy, but also amnesia. What ministers did yesterday is submerged beneath what they promise for tomorrow. So this is the right time to examine not merely the hardening of governmental arteries, familiar enough after four years, but something more sinister: the closing of the gates of self-awareness. Power has done many things to Labour. One of them is to make ministers incapable of seeing that, in immigration policy, they have become what they once abhorred.

The starting point matters. This government came to office with anti-racism among its articles of faith. It abolished the primary-purpose rule, under which migrant spouses were subjected to an especially degrading test of whether marriage was merely a device for entering Britain. It set up the landmark inquiry into the racist murder of Stephen Lawrence, and accepted the language of institutional racism that flowed from it. Leading ministers did not merely profess racial equality as a useful policy. They seemed to embody it. Jack Straw, in particular, located himself in the honourable Labour tradition for which discrimination was not just mistaken but morally repellent.

It was, in a word, unimaginable that these people would do otherwise. This was part of the reassurance they gave. Whatever compromises office required, however many old positions had to be abandoned in the process of modernisation, race was different. Here was a line embedded in the progressive conscience. Labour might become tougher, more managerial, even more authoritarian. But it could not enact racial discrimination. The identity of the ministers guaranteed the outcome.

Now consider what happened. The government passed a Race Relations Act which, while extending protection against discrimination in public functions, explicitly exempted immigration officers acting under ministerial authority. The exemption was not inadvertent. It was the legal gateway through which discrimination could be authorised. A ministerial order then permitted different treatment of people from unspecified nationalities. When this was attacked, the minister responsible called the issue a “technicality”. The criticism was “bizarre”.

Technicality is an interesting word. It is the refuge of ministers who do not want the meaning of their actions examined. Yet the technical provision was exactly where the moral action lay. Without it, immigration officers would have been constrained by the same law Labour was proudly imposing elsewhere. With it, the Home Office acquired statutory power to tell them that race or nationality could determine the treatment an individual received. The exemption was not peripheral to the principle. It destroyed it.

The first order concealed the reach of this power behind official generality. The second did not. It identified the groups. Kurds, Roma, Albanians, Tamils, Pontic Greeks, Somalis and Afghans were among those to be subjected to “more rigorous examination”. Not individuals about whom there was evidence. Not passengers whose documents excited reasonable suspicion. Whole classes of human beings were formally selected because ministers believed members of those classes were more likely to be illegal immigrants or bogus asylum-seekers.

Read the list again. It is hard to improve on its vicious absurdity. Kurds do not possess Kurdish passports. Roma do not arrive bearing documents issued by the state of Romany. Some of the designated categories have no nation-state corresponding to them, which means that officials must discover identity by appearance, speech, name, origin and inference. They must look at a person and decide what kind of person he seems to be. What is this, if not racial profiling? What possible definition of institutional discrimination excludes a statutory instruction to treat an inferred ethnic group more harshly?

The order does not merely permit prejudice to occur at the margin. It organises the prejudice. It takes the crude instinct of the suspicious official and gives it the dignity of law. The applicant ceases to be a person whose case should be judged on its merits. He becomes a Kurd, a Roma, a Tamil: a specimen of a suspect category. Labour ministers who spent years explaining why black citizens should not be presumed criminal now authorise immigration officers to presume foreign ethnic groups fraudulent.

There is a smaller clause, if anything more revealing. Where information necessary for an application is not available in a language the applicant understands, officials need not provide it in one he does. The applicant can therefore be caught by a process he cannot comprehend, then blamed for failing to comply with it. Bureaucracy has many ingenious means of cruelty. Few are more complete than imposing a test while withholding the words in which the test can be understood.

The history of this measure contains two phases of deception. During the bill’s passage, ministers reassured anxious MPs that the power to discriminate would be used benignly. It might, they said, allow favourable treatment for groups such as Kosovan refugees. The exemption was needed to help, not punish. This was a lie. The orders now show the power being used precisely to impose extra suspicion on named ethnic groups. What was sold as positive discrimination became officialised hostility.

Then came phase two. Once the law was safely enacted, the responsible minister explained with breezy candour that the order merely put on a formal basis what immigration officers had long been doing. Why, she seemed to wonder, could anyone object? This was meant as reassurance. In fact it enlarged the offence. If discriminatory selection had been practised informally, the government’s duty under a Race Relations Act was to stop it, not legalise it. Long-standing injustice is not purified by being written down. It becomes systematised.

Three conclusions follow. First, ethnic selection is now official policy. Ministers point to the appointment of a race monitor. But the monitor operates retrospectively, does not investigate individual cases and cannot protect the person standing at the desk when the discriminatory judgment is made. He is a public-relations figurehead, permitted to count the bruises after the blows have fallen. Monitoring injustice is not the same as preventing it.

Second, this is political cowardice. The government has allowed itself to be driven by the asylum rhetoric of the hard opposition right. The opposition says the country is being invaded by frauds, and ministers respond by proving their toughness against categories of foreigner least able to answer back. In doing so, they collaborate in a discourse which treats asylum-seeking itself as presumptive evidence of deceit. The bogus asylum-seeker has become one of the indispensable villains of modern politics, endlessly denounced because he possesses neither vote nor voice.

Third, something has rotted in Labour. Its ministers have become as bad as their predecessors, while losing the intellectual capacity to recognise the fact. The party barely protested. MPs who would once have risen in fury against an ethnic list let it pass as another Home Office instrument. Most startling is the minister herself, a self-proclaimed anti-racist who appears genuinely unable to see the contradiction. This is not hypocrisy in its ordinary, conscious sense. It is more alarming than that.

For power corrupts moral sensibility not always through conspiracy or venality. There is no need to suppose ministers privately assembled to devise a racist law. The deeper corruption lies in righteous self-belief. Because they know themselves to be anti-racist, whatever they do cannot be racist. Because their intentions are decent, the consequences must be defensible. Identity becomes an alibi. Behind this wall of certainty, self-examination expires.

The law is a disgrace. It selects ethnic groups for punishment, licenses officials to infer race, and denies some applicants even the language necessary to understand their predicament. Perhaps only a party so convinced of its own anti-racism would have had the nerve to smuggle such a measure past the line separating decency from shame.

author: Jonathan Freedland
title: cross_topic_1/test/3/test_127
body:
Late at night, when the schedules have run out of confidence and almost everyone has gone to bed, Israeli television sometimes turns back on itself. Up come the old black-and-white anniversary specials: men in work shirts, women in peasant skirts, a kibbutz choir arranged in tidy rows, singing patriotic Hebrew folk melodies to an audience beaming with uncomplicated pride.

It is easy to laugh. The smiles are too broad, the choreography too earnest, the whole thing a socialist-realist tableau served with accordion accompaniment. Yet as Israel celebrates its 60th birthday with street parties, fireworks and beach barbecues, those cheesy programmes offer something more valuable than nostalgia. They provide a window into what Israel was, what it imagined itself to be and what it has become.

That contrast matters because both Israel's enemies and its friends often misunderstand it. The former cast its birth as an imperial project, a European intrusion into the Middle East. The latter can be so busy defending Israel against that charge that they fail to see how far the country has travelled from its founding ideals. Israel was created not as a luxury colony but as a desperate refuge for a stateless people. Yet the collectivist society built by those refugees has retreated into a privatised bubble, losing along the way the urgency it once attached to peace.

Start with the standard indictment. Israel, we are told, was a western implant, a white settler state comparable to Australia, Rhodesia or South Africa: Europeans arrived, took the land and established an enclave sustained by superior arms and outside power.

There is enough Palestinian dispossession in the story to make that charge emotionally potent. But it is also too simple. Look at the Jewish population of Israel now: Moroccan, Russian, Ethiopian, Kurdish, Iraqi, Yemenite, Polish. This is not a transplanted fragment of Belgium or Surrey. Many Israeli Jews came not from the imperial capitals of Europe but from Muslim lands in which their families had lived for centuries, leaving because they were frightened, expelled or no longer welcome.

One of Israel's unofficial anthems captures the conviction beneath that history: Ein Li Eretz Acheret, I have no other land. The phrase is moving precisely because it is not uniquely Jewish. Palestinians feel the same way. There lies the tragic symmetry of the conflict: two peoples, each convinced that this is the one place on earth that cannot be exchanged for another.

Still, chronology matters. The Jewish state was established just three years after the liberation of Auschwitz, when the world had finally been confronted with the consequences of Jewish homelessness. The lesson was not abstract. It was that a people without a state had no door it could be sure would open, no government obliged to rescue it, no patch of ground on which it could claim the elementary right to live.

Consider Yossi Harel, captain of the Exodus. In 1947 his ship carried 4,500 Holocaust survivors towards Palestine. These were not adventurers seeking fresh territory or businessmen scouting for minerals. They were survivors of the greatest machinery of murder Europe had devised. Yet the British turned them back, eventually sending them to Germany, of all places.

The moral distinction is not difficult to grasp. The pioneers of America, Africa and Australasia often had a home already. They sought a second one, or land, labour and resources. The Jewish refugees of the 1940s were different. They resembled not the landlord looking to enlarge his estate but the homeless man desperate for a roof. That does not erase the catastrophe suffered by Palestinians. It does explain why the Jews came and why they believed they had nowhere else to go.

The old television choirs reveal something else. Early Israel was much less like Rhodesia than East Germany: a small, austere country in which socialism functioned as a state religion. The kibbutz was its purest shrine, but the collectivist impulse reached everywhere.

Homes were fitted with the same mass-produced floor tiles. One kibbutz manufactured every toilet seat in the country. Foreign investors could be required to surrender a 51% stake in their companies to the Histadrut, Israel's trade union congress. The message was clear: private wealth would be tolerated, perhaps, but the collective came first.

That Israel has vanished. The kibbutzim themselves have privatised. Members now own their homes, earn different wages and make individual provision for their own futures. The communal dining hall, once the centre of life, can feel like a relic.

The kibbutz is a metaphor for the country. Israeli society has not merely embraced the market; its citizens have withdrawn from the collective sphere. They have built what they call a bu'ah, a bubble: the private zone of family, career, café and holiday that screens out the anxiety of national life.

The polling captures the contradiction. Nearly 40% of Israelis believe their country faces a serious threat of destruction. Yet 83% say they are satisfied with their personal lives. Existential dread and private contentment coexist. The nation may be perched above an abyss, but dinner is booked for nine and the children are doing well at school.

That mood hangs over the 60th anniversary and over the Annapolis peace process. The US administration wants visible progress before George W Bush's visit, imposing one more deadline on a conflict already littered with expired timetables.

Israel insists it is trying. Foreign minister Tzipi Livni speaks proudly of continuing negotiations with the Palestinians even on “days of terror”. Prime minister Ehud Olmert's hawkish critics make the opposite accusation: that his diplomatic efforts are merely smoke, designed to distract from a corruption inquiry.

But the problem is not that Israel is secretly negotiating too much. It is that there is no genuine urgency behind the talks. When Jimmy Carter came to Jerusalem carrying messages from Hamas and Damascus, not one frontline Israeli minister would meet him. The official argument was that engagement with Hamas would legitimise the Islamists and undermine the Palestinian president, Mahmoud Abbas.

That might be convincing if Olmert were doing anything serious to strengthen Abbas. He could remove illegal West Bank outposts. He could offer compensation to settlers willing to leave voluntarily. He could show Palestinians that moderation yields something more tangible than another meeting and another joint statement. He has done none of those things with the force the moment requires.

Israelis have an answer, and it is not frivolous. They withdrew from Lebanon in 2000 and received Katyushas from Hezbollah. They withdrew from Gaza in 2005 and received Qassams from Hamas. To many Israelis the lesson is brutally straightforward: give up territory and the vacated ground becomes a launchpad.

That conclusion has produced a deep cynicism. Why take another risk for peace when the last risks brought rockets? Why trust Palestinian promises when Hamas controls Gaza? Why uproot settlements if the reward may be missiles landing closer to Tel Aviv?

These questions deserve answers. But they cannot become an alibi for permanent inaction. For now, Israelis believe they can manage without peace. The separation barrier has sharply contained terrorism, allowing daily life to resume. The economy is buoyant. Restaurants are full. The so-called demographic threat—the prospect that Arabs will eventually outnumber Jews in the territory Israel controls—seems distant and mathematical, too abstract to compete with the immediate comfort of the bubble.

That may be the most dangerous change of all. Israelis once pursued peace because the conflict intruded relentlessly into ordinary life. Now they can avert their eyes. The status quo remains unbearable for Palestinians, but for many Israelis it has become bearable enough. And a bearable conflict can last for ever—or at least until it explodes.

The birthday celebrations will be loud. With economic growth running at 5.1%, there is plenty of money for parties, beach food and drink. Israel at 60 is dynamic, inventive and more prosperous than its founders could have imagined. Its citizens have much to celebrate, including the astonishing fact of survival.

But survival is not the same as fulfilment. The old television programmes, for all their absurdity, preserve a memory of people who believed they were engaged in a common enterprise. Their Israel was poorer and greyer, burdened with hypocrisies of its own, but it possessed a collective purpose that today's privatised nation struggles to recover.

Soon the fireworks will end and the barbecues will cool. Long after midnight, perhaps only a few insomniacs will still be watching as those kibbutz singers reappear, swaying in monochrome. They will hear a melody whose idealism sounds more passé now than ever: Shir L'shalom, the song for peace.

author: Jonathan Freedland
title: cross_topic_1/test/3/test_22
body:
Apologies are often easier to give than to receive. The one who says sorry gets to speak first, choosing the words, setting the terms and perhaps claiming a patch of moral high ground. The recipient has no such freedom. They may still be angry, still too raw to forgive. Yet if they refuse the offered hand, they risk seeming bitter or obstinate: the injured party suddenly cast as the problem.

Scale that awkward scene up from the kitchen table to Northern Ireland and you have some sense of the difficulty created by the IRA’s apology this week. Republicans can point to their statement as evidence of movement, even humanity. Unionists, and especially the bereaved, are left with a harder question. Is this a genuine act of contrition, deserving recognition? Or is it merely another manoeuvre in the long, exhausting chess game of the peace process?

The statement itself was striking. Its vocabulary owed more to the psychotherapist’s consulting room than to the traditional communiqués of the IRA army council. It spoke of “hurt”, “pain” and “grief”, acknowledging the suffering caused by republican violence. Notably, it did not immediately reach for the usual alibi, blaming Britain and the British government for everything that followed. There was no instant retreat into the familiar argument that the conflict had been imposed on republicans, leaving them no choice but armed struggle.

And yet the apology was tightly drawn. It was directed at “non-combatants”, a category that leaves outside its embrace the police officers and soldiers killed by the IRA. The organisation still regards those men as legitimate targets in a war. Its sorrow, therefore, has limits.

The timing was not accidental. The statement came as Northern Ireland approached the 30th anniversary of Bloody Friday, July 21 1972, when the IRA planted more than 20 bombs across Belfast, killing nine people and injuring scores. The day remains one of the most terrible entries in the Troubles’ vast ledger of horror: explosions coming so fast that the emergency services could barely keep up, ordinary people caught in a city transformed into a killing ground.

The first reactions exposed the division inside unionism. Tom Donnelly, whose sister died on Bloody Friday, said he was moved. He wanted to believe that the statement represented a step forward, perhaps even the beginning of something better. His response deserves respect, not least because those who suffered most have earned the right to judge such words for themselves.

But most unionists were scornful. “Pure PR” was the common verdict. They saw not repentance but choreography, an intervention calculated to help Sinn Féin through the latest crisis. For another deadline is approaching. Next Wednesday the British government is required to declare whether the IRA is maintaining its ceasefire. If the verdict is no, David Trimble will demand that Sinn Féin be expelled from Northern Ireland’s power-sharing executive. Suddenly an apology issued days before that judgment looks less like spontaneous remorse and more like evidence submitted for the defence.

Unionist cynicism has not come from nowhere. Many of them always regarded the 1998 Good Friday agreement as a moral outrage. It put men linked to an armed movement into government, while releasing prisoners convicted of terrible crimes. Unionists swallowed that bitter medicine only because they believed it would lead republicans permanently away from violence and into democratic politics.

That belief has collapsed. First came the arrest in Colombia of three republicans accused of links with Farc guerrillas. Then there was the break-in at Castlereagh police station, an operation whose sophistication inevitably prompted suspicion of republican involvement. Meanwhile, the streets of Belfast have remained violent, with rioting and intimidation mocking the notion that the Troubles are safely over.

Taken together, these episodes have hollowed out unionist faith in the agreement. If elections to the assembly were held now, Ian Paisley’s anti-agreement Democratic Unionist party would probably overwhelm Trimble’s Ulster Unionists. The man who signed the accord would be punished; the man who denounced it would inherit unionism.

That explains Trimble’s current demand for an American envoy, appointed by George W Bush, to assess whether the IRA is honouring its ceasefire. Unionists believe an outside monitor, especially one carrying Washington’s authority, would reassure their increasingly sceptical voters. At the very least, it might buy Trimble time.

Republicans reject the idea with the jesuitical rigour they bring to anything not explicitly contained in the Good Friday agreement. There is no provision for such an envoy, they say, and therefore there should be none. Nor will they accept any “widening” of the definition of the ceasefire to include activity beyond the narrow question of whether the IRA is shooting and bombing.

They confidently expect Tony Blair to deliver a verdict ambiguous enough to keep Sinn Féin in government. And it is hard not to notice the choreographed quality of events: first the IRA statement, then supportive noises from London and Dublin, all ahead of Wednesday’s decision. The machinery of the peace process can sometimes resemble a stage production whose actors insist they have never met, even as they hit their cues with perfect timing.

Still, unionists should not dismiss the apology altogether. For the IRA, this was a big deal. A self-styled liberation movement does not find it easy to admit that its campaign caused wrongful suffering. It believes its cause was just and its war legitimate. Until recently Sinn Féin representatives could barely express regret without wrapping it in qualifications. Now the IRA itself has used the word apology. That is movement.

Try a thought experiment. Imagine September 2031, the 30th anniversary of the destruction of the World Trade Centre. Suppose al-Qaida’s ruling council issued a statement expressing regret for the pain caused to the “non-combatant” victims of September 11. Would that heal the families of the dead? Of course not. Many would regard the words as grotesquely inadequate. Some would reject them with fury.

But alter the circumstances. Imagine that al-Qaida had maintained a ceasefire for six or eight years, had entered a political process and had largely abandoned attacks. Would the world not feel some relief at such a statement? Would it not reveal something about the organisation’s present intention, even if it could not redeem its past?

That is the proper test here. The IRA’s words cannot undo Bloody Friday, Enniskillen or any of the other atrocities that scar Northern Ireland’s memory. Nor is the apology complete. It should have included “combatants” too. The RUC constable shot on his doorstep and the young soldier blown up on patrol had families who knew hurt, pain and grief no less than anyone else.

But those crying “ploy” should ask what sinister purpose these ploys are serving. The answer is: to keep republicans inside government, exactly where the agreement allows them to be. Decommissioning was also dismissed as a stunt. Yet the weapons put beyond use cannot kill. An apology may be tactical, but its effect is to push the IRA further into the language and habits of politics.

None of this requires innocence about republican motives. Sinn Féin and the IRA have not embraced the peace process out of sudden affection for unionism. They have decided it serves their strategic interest. But peace processes often depend on former enemies concluding that peace suits them better than war.

Republicanism is on a slow, incomplete journey from a paramilitary past to a political future. It is not neat or perfect, but more real with each passing month. Unionists are entitled to demand proof, vigilance and more movement. But they should also recognise movement when it comes. In peace processes, as in life, sometimes you must know how to say yes.

author: Jonathan Freedland
title: cross_topic_1/test/3/test_25
body:
“If it ain’t broke, don’t fix it.” For generations, that has been the most effective argument in defence of the British constitution. Never mind that it is not written down in one place, that it is instead scattered across statutes, conventions, customs and understandings, some ancient, some improvised, some barely understood. Never mind that no ordinary citizen could lay hands on a single document and discover the rules by which he or she is governed. The system has worked, its defenders say. It has endured wars, revolutions abroad, industrialisation and the loss of empire. Why meddle with machinery that has kept ticking for centuries?

It is a powerful argument, appealing to the British preference for experience over theory, the practical over the abstract. It casts constitutional reformers as dangerous mechanics, eager to dismantle a serviceable engine merely because its parts do not look neat on paper.

But there is an obvious answer. The machine is no longer working. It is not merely creaking or showing its age. It is broken.

The test of a constitution is not whether it possesses picturesque traditions or whether foreign lawyers admire its flexibility. The test is the quality of government it produces. Does it restrain power? Does it expose bad decisions before they become disasters? Does it ensure that those who govern are answerable to those who are governed? By those measures, the British system is failing.

Consider the record of the past 15 years. Start with the pensions scandal, which saw hundreds of thousands persuaded to abandon secure occupational schemes for private alternatives that left them poorer. Add BSE, where official complacency allowed a crisis in agriculture to become a terror in the national imagination. Put parliamentary sleaze next on the list: cash for questions, brown envelopes and the spectacle of MPs behaving as if public office were a private franchise.

Keep going. Railway privatisation took a unified national service and shattered it into a labyrinth of operators, regulators, contractors and subcontractors, leaving responsibility everywhere and nowhere. The poll tax was not merely an unpopular policy but an act of governmental folly so vast that it provoked riots, destroyed a prime minister and had to be abandoned. And now the Millennium Dome, intended as a gleaming emblem of a modern Britain, stands instead as a monument to confused purpose, opaque decision-making and the political conviction that one more injection of public money will somehow make everything all right.

These episodes are usually treated separately. The pension scandal belongs on the financial pages, BSE to agriculture, sleaze to parliamentary gossip, rail privatisation to transport. The poll tax is remembered as Thatcherism’s final convulsion; the dome as New Labour’s expensive embarrassment. Different ministers, different departments, different governments.

But look again and a common thread appears. In each case, power was concentrated among a small circle. Scrutiny came late, if at all. Parliament failed to halt the damage. Ministers were able to hide behind civil servants, advisers, commercial confidentiality or one another. By the time the truth emerged, the policy was entrenched, the money spent or the victims already harmed.

Two major inquiries have supplied the official confirmation. The Scott inquiry into arms sales to Iraq exposed a culture in which ministers could mislead parliament while maintaining that no lie had technically been told. The Phillips report on BSE revealed not one uniquely wicked individual but a governmental structure incapable of sharing information, testing assumptions or admitting uncertainty. In both cases, the tempting response was to hunt for a culprit. Yet the deeper verdict was more disturbing: the system itself had failed.

Britain’s constitution grants the executive extraordinary power. A government commanding a Commons majority can, in practice, do almost anything. The Commons is meant to check ministers, but those ministers usually control it through party loyalty and patronage. The second chamber is unelected. The courts, until the Human Rights Act, had only a limited role in restraining parliament. Local government exists at Westminster’s pleasure. The prime minister can choose the election date, appoint peers, make treaties and deploy forces abroad through powers inherited from the crown.

We congratulate ourselves that these powers are used moderately. But a constitution should not depend on the good manners of those who govern. It should be built for the day when they are mistaken, arrogant or simply overwhelmed.

The public seems to have reached that conclusion before the political class. Confidence in the House of Commons halved between 1985 and 1995. A 1996 European Union poll placed British faith in parliament close to the bottom of the member-state league. That should have shocked a country raised on the belief that Westminster is the mother of parliaments. Instead, the figures were noted, filed away and forgotten.

Turnout tells the same story more brutally. Participation in local elections has collapsed, with wards routinely electing councillors on derisory votes. A Leeds byelection attracted fewer than one voter in five. Politicians often respond by blaming apathy, as if millions of citizens had simultaneously become too lazy to walk to a polling station. But perhaps non-voters are making a political judgment. Perhaps they have noticed that power has moved away from the institutions they are invited to elect.

A Mori survey found that 71% of first-time voters believed their ballot would make little or no difference. That is not apathy. It is a rational response to powerlessness. Citizens are voting with their feet, walking away from a system that seems to hear them only once every four or five years and often not even then.

The traditional defence of the constitution relied not only on its supposed effectiveness but on its mystique. It was a delicate fabric, we were told, woven over centuries. Pull one thread and the whole thing might unravel. Better to leave it untouched.

Since 1997, Labour has destroyed that argument. Devolution to Scotland and Wales, the new arrangements in Northern Ireland and the first-stage reform of the House of Lords amount to constitutional change on a scale for which historians have to reach back to the 1830s to find a precedent. The constitution was not too sacred to touch after all. Its rules could be altered by ordinary politicians passing ordinary laws.

That is a welcome discovery. But Labour’s reforms have also created a new danger. The patchwork quilt is already coming apart, without any agreed design for what should replace it. Half-reform can be worse than none, because it exposes contradictions while leaving them unresolved.

Four Nations

Devolution has forced Britain to confront a truth it preferred to blur. This is not one nation but four, held together in a union that has operated as if it were a unitary state governed from Westminster. Scotland now has a parliament with substantial control over its domestic affairs. Wales has an assembly. Northern Ireland has institutions rooted in a carefully negotiated division of power. Britain has begun to acquire the shape of a federation, while refusing to use the word.

But what of England? Is Westminster now the federal legislature of the United Kingdom or does it double as England’s parliament? If it does both, how can it perform either role fairly? Why should Scotland possess powers denied to Wales? Why should English regions have less autonomy than either?

Then comes the West Lothian question, once dismissed as an ingenious parliamentary riddle and now a democratic grievance. Why should a Scottish MP vote on schools or hospitals in England when an English MP has no vote on those matters in Scotland? There may be answers, but the current settlement does not provide them.

Some complain that devolution opened a can of worms. But the worms were always there. The can is merely open now, and shutting our eyes will not put them back.

A written constitution would begin by defining the union: which powers belong to the centre, which to the nations and regions, and which cannot be reclaimed by Westminster on a whim. It would decide whether England needs its own parliament or whether English power should be devolved to regions and cities. Above all, it would replace ambiguity with consent.

Without that settlement, resentment will grow in every direction. Scots will suspect Westminster of plotting to claw back power. English voters will believe they are financing privileges they do not enjoy. Welsh devolution will remain an anaemic imitation of Scotland’s. The union will not be strengthened by vagueness. It may unravel because of it.

Shattered Mystique

The House of Lords provides the clearest example of the danger of incomplete reform. Removing most hereditary peers was necessary. Membership of parliament should not pass from father to son like an antique watch. But the change has not solved the problem of the second chamber. It has illuminated it.

When the Lords was dominated by hereditary peers, it could be dismissed as a Ruritanian joke: ermine, titles and elderly aristocrats wandering in to defend foxhunting. Its absurdity provided its own explanation. Now Britons look at a supposedly reformed chamber and see something harder to justify: not a single member elected by the public.

The remaining peers sit there by appointment, accident of clerical office or temporary hereditary reprieve. The government has removed the old rationale without supplying a democratic one. A chamber that once lacked legitimacy because it was ancient now lacks legitimacy because it is modern and still unelected.

The old Tory position—leave well alone—is dead. Once the constitution has been opened up, it must be repaired properly. That means a second chamber with a clear purpose, defined powers and democratic authority. It may represent the nations and regions, as federal chambers do elsewhere. It may be elected by proportional representation, providing a counterweight to the distorted majorities of the Commons. But appointment by prime ministerial favour cannot be the final answer.

Even William Hague’s Conservatives are flirting with ideas once regarded as dangerously radical, including an English parliament. That tells us something. Constitutional reform is no longer the hobby of a few liberal eccentrics. It has become unavoidable.

The risk is that Britain will settle for a hybrid: old custom draped in New Labour modernisation, ancient powers exercised by media-age politicians, devolution without federalism and a reformed Lords without elections. We could end up with the worst of both worlds—the obscurity of the old order combined with the instability of the new.

Nor is this crisis occurring in isolation. Next month European leaders will gather at Nice to debate the future shape of the European Union. Behind the technical language lies a set of fundamental questions. Should more decisions be taken by majority voting or should national vetoes remain? What does the common currency mean for economic independence? Can Europe possess a shared military force? Does the union need a basic law, perhaps even a constitution of its own?

These are all arguments about sovereignty: who has power, where it comes from and how it can be controlled.

The same debate is visible beyond Europe. The demonstrators who filled the streets of Seattle and Prague, protesting against global capitalism, were asking who rules the world. Elected governments? Ordinary people? Or corporations and institutions such as the World Trade Organisation, operating beyond the reach of a national ballot box?

Again the question is sovereignty. Who should be in charge?

It is the question for the world, for Europe and for Britain. Yet Britain enters this great argument unable to explain where sovereignty lies within its own borders. Is it vested in parliament, the crown, the government, the courts, the Scottish people or the British people as a whole? The answer shifts according to circumstance. What is praised as flexibility in quiet times becomes confusion in a crisis.

Three forces are now converging. The first is the repeated failure of the old system to provide accountable, competent government. The second is Labour’s constitutional revolution, bold enough to dismantle the past but too timid to complete the future. The third is the global struggle over sovereignty, forcing every country to decide what powers it can share and which must remain under democratic control.

Together they make the case for a new constitutional settlement. Not a lawyers’ ornament or an elegant document to be admired behind glass, but a practical division of power: a bill of rights citizens can enforce; a parliament capable of holding the executive to account; an elected second chamber; fixed terms; defined powers for nations, regions and local government; and clear rules governing war, treaties, appointments and the conduct of ministers.

A written constitution would not prevent every blunder. No document can abolish stupidity or guarantee wise leadership. But it could make error harder, scrutiny stronger and responsibility clearer. It would complete Labour’s unfinished business and give the four nations a reason to remain together. It would allow Britain to negotiate its place in Europe and the world after first deciding who governs Britain itself.

The old answer—if it ain’t broke—no longer works, because the evidence of breakage is all around us: in failed policies, empty polling stations, mistrusted institutions and a union uncertain of its shape. The moth-eaten garb of the past no longer fits. A new century and a new millennium demand something better: a constitution made not by inheritance or accident, but by citizens—and written down for all to see.

author: Jonathan Freedland
title: cross_topic_1/train/3/train_27
body:
Schools have Ofsted, hospitals have star ratings and corporations submit their executives to the annual indignity of the performance review. A columnist should surely be no exception. If ministers must be held to account for promises made, targets missed and disasters mysteriously attributed to “communications”, then those who pass judgment on them ought occasionally to face the same ordeal.

Exactly one year ago, this column offered six reckless predictions for the 12 months ahead. The adjective was meant as insurance, a verbal crash helmet to be worn in the event of collision with reality. Now comes the appraisal: a chance to see where the crystal ball worked, where it misted up and where it should have been hurled through the nearest window.

Start with Hutton. The prediction was that Tony Blair would survive and that the BBC would be faulted for the Andrew Gilligan report and its defective editorial procedures. So far, so good. The error was to assume that Lord Hutton would “spread the pain evenly”, censuring the government for the way David Kelly’s name emerged while chastising the corporation for its journalism.

That turned out to be a touching act of faith. Hutton did not spread the pain evenly so much as tip the entire bucket over the BBC. The government emerged not merely alive but gleaming, while the corporation lost its chairman and director general. Most strikingly, Hutton went further than expected on the central controversy, pronouncing on the charge that the government had sexed up its Iraq dossier with a boldness few had anticipated. I had assumed an unelected judge would hesitate before stepping too deeply on to political ground. He did not hesitate. He strode on, planted a flag and left.

Verdict: half-right on the outcome, wholly wrong on the distribution of blame. Two marks out of four.

Next, America. The prediction named George Bush as the winner of November’s presidential election. That was correct. Unfortunately, it also identified his Democratic opponent as Howard Dean, which is rather like correctly predicting the winner of the FA Cup final while naming the other team as Torquay.

Dean was supposed to dominate Iowa and New Hampshire, brush aside a southern challenge from Wesley Clark and arrive at the convention as the insurgent nominee. Instead he came third in Iowa, delivered one over-amplified yelp to his supporters and was swiftly transformed from internet-age phenomenon into late-night comedy material. John Kerry, whom the experts had pronounced all but dead, won the nomination and came within one state of the White House.

Verdict: right president, wrong opponent. Since elections generally require two candidates, one mark out of two.

Then Iraq. Here the forecast began well. Violence against the occupation did indeed continue, puncturing the hope that Saddam Hussein’s capture would end the resistance. The man in the spider hole had not, after all, been directing every roadside bomb and ambush. The insurgency survived him and grew deadlier.

But the rest was fantasy. I expected the Bush administration, anxious about election-year opinion, to arrange Iraqi elections by the summer and begin bringing troops home, ensuring that television showed returning soldiers rather than returning coffins. Instead there were no such elections and no such homecoming. The occupation deepened; Abu Ghraib became the defining image; Falluja entered the vocabulary of war. Bush chose not to disguise the commitment but to defend it, persuading enough Americans that withdrawal would amount to defeat.

Verdict: right about the violence, wrong about the politics. One out of three.

On Israel and Palestine, the prediction was that Israelis would increasingly conclude Ariel Sharon’s old strategy had failed. That much happened. The belief that force alone could suppress Palestinian resistance lost ground, while the demographic argument for separation gained strength.

What I entirely failed to foresee was that Sharon himself would act on that conclusion. I imagined the old warrior being pushed aside, perhaps after corruption allegations engulfed him, and replaced by Binyamin Netanyahu.
