Instead Sharon announced his own plan to withdraw from Gaza, turning against the settlers who had once regarded him as their champion. The bulldozer did not leave the road; it abruptly changed direction.

Nor did the scandals force him from office. They swirled, darkened and occasionally threatened, but Sharon remained where he was. Once again, the prediction assumed an accountability mechanism would operate in a familiar way: accusation, investigation, resignation. Once again, it did not.

Verdict: correctly spotted the change in national mood, entirely missed the man who would harness it. One out of three.

What of the Conservatives? Michael Howard was forecast to have a solid year, helped by June’s European elections. Technically, that was right. The Tories topped a nationwide poll, a feat that brought relief to a party that had spent years behaving like a once-grand department store permanently holding a closing-down sale.

But examine the figure and the triumph shrinks. The Conservatives won with a vote share so low it would once have been considered catastrophic. Their success rested less on a surge of popular affection than on Labour’s collapse, the rise of Ukip and a turnout that hardly suggested the nation had been electrified by Howard’s leadership. To call it a solid year is defensible, but only in the sense that congealed porridge is solid.

Verdict: correct in the small print, misleading in the headline. Half a mark.

The final domestic forecasts were kinder. Ken Livingstone was indeed readmitted to Labour, reversing the expulsion that had followed his independent mayoral bid, and he duly won a second term at City Hall. The prodigal son returned, though without displaying much penitence on the journey home.

And Gordon Brown stayed at the Treasury and waited. He had another year of being indispensable, formidable and not prime minister. The chancellor’s patience has become one of the fixed features of British politics, like rain at Wimbledon or Liberal Democrat promises of a breakthrough. Each year brings fresh speculation that the prize is imminent; each year ends with Brown still next door.

Verdict: two clean hits. The forecaster briefly removes the dunce’s cap.

What lessons emerge from this audit? The first is that political insiders are not always wise guides. The Dean prediction reflected the settled view of an impressive number of consultants, journalists and Democratic professionals who had convinced themselves that his money, organisation and online following made him unstoppable. They were not merely wrong; they were wrong together, which somehow made the error feel respectable. It should not have.

The second lesson is more surprising, at least for a journalist: I was not cynical enough. Again and again, I expected institutions of accountability to function fairly and predictably. I assumed Hutton would share blame between government and broadcaster. I assumed scandal would remove Sharon. I assumed failure in Iraq would compel a timetable for withdrawal. In each case, the people or institutions at the top proved more durable than the evidence against them.

That points to the defining theme of the year: unaccountability. Intelligence on Iraq was discredited, prisoners were abused, military planning was exposed as disastrously inadequate, yet scarcely anyone senior paid a price. The powerful absorbed the blow and carried on. Responsibility was everywhere, but consequences were nowhere.

So 2004 offers two lessons. Do not expect the machinery of accountability to work. And do not make predictions.

That last rule is the safer one. After all, who could have forecast a year ending with the home secretary, David Blunkett, embroiled in an affair, a paternity battle and a row over the fast-tracked visa of his lover’s nanny? Some events lie beyond analysis. The crystal ball has its limits; occasionally it just falls off the mantelpiece.

author: Jonathan Freedland
title: cross_topic_1/train/3/train_31
body:
The guardians of civic probity have spoken, and the nation can relax. Sir Anthony Hammond has examined the affair of the Hinduja brothers, their British passports and Peter Mandelson, and found nothing “improper”. The billionaire donors to the Millennium Dome did not, it seems, buy anything so vulgar as citizenship. A senior cabinet minister merely took an interest; officials merely hurried things along. Nothing to see here.

Nor need we worry about Keith Vaz. The parliamentary committee on standards and privileges cleared the former Europe minister of nearly all the allegations against him. It found one minor breach, but declined to punish him for it. True, the committee had been forced to abandon much of its investigation because Vaz refused to cooperate. But that only proves how scrupulous the process was: unable to obtain answers, it wisely stopped asking questions.

And Downing Street’s intervention in a planning matter affecting the billionaire arms broker Wafic Said? Again, innocent. Number 10 was not trying to influence the merits of the application, merely expressing concern over its “timing”. So that’s all right then. Passports, parliamentary standards, planning: on every count the official machinery has whirred, clicked and produced the same reassuring verdict. Nothing improper occurred. All is well.

Except it does not feel well. The reports may be clean, the committees satisfied and the ministers technically acquitted, but the odour remains. If the rules say that nothing wrong happened, why does it still smell wrong?

Forget for a moment the precise allegations, the denials, the finely parsed distinctions between pressure and interest, intervention and inquiry. Apply instead the test used by ordinary people, the ones who do not have lawyers to explain that the thing plainly before their eyes is not really there. These cases are all versions of the same story. Someone had money and received special treatment.

The Hammond report itself supplies the evidence. It records that the passport application of Srichand Hinduja was personally championed by the head of the immigration and nationality directorate. It shows that residency rules were interpreted flexibly so that the process could move faster. It recounts the supporting efforts of a glittering roster of former prime ministers and senior ministers, men whose telephone calls are returned and whose letters do not languish in an in-tray. Edward Heath backed the Hindujas. James Callaghan spoke for them. Margaret Thatcher offered support. Peter Mandelson raised the case inside government. This extraordinary mobilisation, the report concludes, was “proper”.

Perhaps it was, under the definition available to Sir Anthony Hammond. But that definition is precisely the problem. An ordinary applicant who had not helped rescue the government’s flagship Dome project would not have enjoyed such a procession of grandees. He would have filled in the form, waited in the queue and been told that rules were rules. The Hindujas were allowed access to a different system: one with doors, corridors and helpful people at the end of a telephone.

We have seen this film before. Bernie Ecclestone donated £1m to Labour; Formula One was subsequently spared the full force of the government’s tobacco advertising ban. Ministers insisted the two events were unrelated, a coincidence as pure as driven snow. Michael Ashcroft poured millions into the Conservative party and was rewarded with a peerage, after some awkwardness over his tax affairs. Again, defenders could assemble a technical case showing that every box had eventually been ticked. Yet the public grasped the larger truth. Vast wealth does not always purchase a specific favour, complete with invoice and receipt. It buys proximity, attention and speed. It ensures that your problem becomes someone important’s problem.

What can be done? The first answer usually offered is state funding of political parties. Remove the need for politicians to grovel before millionaires, the argument runs, and they will no longer feel obliged to reward them. There is force in that. No party leader should have to spend evenings flattering tycoons in the hope that one of them will sign a seven-figure cheque.

But state funding would not have prevented the Hinduja affair. Their money was not going to Labour; it was going to the Millennium Dome. The leverage came not from party finance but from the ability to help a government project that ministers desperately wanted to succeed. Besides, public funding has dangers of its own. If the established parties decide how the state’s money is distributed, they could starve new movements of the resources needed to challenge them. A reform intended to cleanse democracy could end up freezing it.

What about tougher parliamentary regulation? The Vaz case provides the answer. The standards committee could investigate only so far as the minister was willing to assist it. When he refused, it found itself powerless and dropped most of the charges. A watchdog that can bark only with the permission of the person it is watching is not much of a watchdog. Parliament cannot reliably police this offence because parliamentarians are implicated in it. The system is being asked to cure a disease from which it suffers.

So perhaps we need to define the offence differently. The central wrong here is not a murky exchange of cash for a passport, a peerage or a planning decision. It is the denial of a basic principle: that all citizens should stand equal before the law. What angers people is the conviction that there is one rule for the rich and another for everyone else.

The United States has long had a legal weapon against such behaviour. The 14th amendment to the constitution guarantees every citizen the “equal protection of the laws”. From Brown v Board of Education in 1954 onwards, that clause became the foundation of civil rights litigation. When legislators lacked the courage to dismantle discrimination, citizens went to court. They asked judges to rule that the state could not favour one group and disadvantage another.

Britain now has an instrument with similar potential. Article 14 of the European convention on human rights, absorbed into domestic law by the Human Rights Act, prohibits discrimination in the enjoyment of convention rights on grounds including sex, race, colour and “any other status”. Those last three words matter. Why should “other status” not include wealth?

Imagine two ordinary citizens asking a court why their passport applications took years while a billionaire’s was sped through the system. Imagine a small developer demanding to know why his planning decision remained trapped in bureaucracy while Wafic Said’s case prompted concern in Downing Street. Or a modest business owner asking why the government denied him an exemption from a new policy while Bernie Ecclestone’s Formula One received one.

The cases would not be easy. Keir Starmer, one of the country’s leading human rights lawyers, confirms that discrimination against those with less money could fall within Article 14. But a claimant would need evidence. He would have to prove that the cases were genuinely comparable in every respect except wealth, and show that the difference in treatment could not be objectively justified.

That is a high hurdle, but not an impossible one. And even an unsuccessful challenge would achieve something valuable. It would force the relevant authority to explain itself in open court. No longer would “timing” or “an expression of interest” suffice. Ministers and officials would have to say why one application moved swiftly while another crawled, why one citizen attracted prime ministerial attention while another received a standard letter.

Experts always said the Human Rights Act would make a profound difference to British life. In America, the Bill of Rights has repeatedly advanced causes that politicians found too difficult, dangerous or embarrassing to touch. Now it may be Britain’s turn. For those sick of fast tracks, private telephone calls and special favours for the rich, the political system is too compromised to provide the answer. It is time to go to court.

author: Martin Kettle
title: cross_topic_1/test/4/test_132
body:
When I arrived in Washington as a correspondent, I asked the late Alistair Cooke what British journalists most often failed to understand about the American political system. His answer was immediate. It was not the presidency, Congress, money or the sheer continental scale of American elections. It was the courts. British people, Cooke said, simply did not grasp how important judges were in the government of the United States.

It was good advice, and I thought of it again this week. Partly this was because John McCain was in London to deliver the first Alistair Cooke memorial lecture. Partly it was because George Bush was crossing the Atlantic for the G8 summit. Yet the event dominating political Washington had nothing to do with Africa, global poverty, climate change or Iraq. It concerned the retirement of a 75-year-old judge.

The belief that Britain and America see the world through the same eyes is one of the most persistent deceptions in our national life. The common language makes the two countries seem more alike than they are. Cooke understood the difference. Nowhere is it more revealing than in the contrasting ways the two countries regard their senior judges.

The resignation of Sandra Day O’Connor from the United States supreme court over the Fourth of July weekend was not merely a big Washington story. It was a potentially seismic national event. In its own context, the choice of her successor may be at least as important as the election of a president or a new Congress. Anyone who thinks that is an exaggeration has not understood the American constitution.

The supreme court is not an ornamental committee of venerable lawyers. Its nine members form one of the central institutions of American government. Their rulings define the permissible boundaries of political action. They determine what presidents, legislatures, states, schools, churches, companies and citizens may do. The court can restrain elected power, but it can also enlarge it. Its decisions may endure for generations.

No justice has been nominated for 11 years, an unusually long interval. O’Connor’s departure matters particularly because, for the past decade and a half, she has so often been the court’s deciding vote. She was no liberal in any British meaning of the word. But nor was she a judicial revolutionary. Her cautious, pragmatic judgments helped to decide cases affecting abortion, race, religion and the relationship between the individual and the state. Millions of Americans have lived under rules that were, in decisive part, Sandra Day O’Connor’s rules.

That is why the American right sees her retirement as an immense opportunity, while the left sees it as a moment of direst threat. It has long been an open secret that powerful Republican lobbies regard the reshaping of the supreme court as one of their highest priorities. Their ideal is a court remade under the intellectual leadership of Antonin Scalia in an aggressively conservative mould.

The ambition extends well beyond overturning particular decisions on abortion or religion. At its largest, it seeks to dismantle much of the role accumulated by the federal government since at least the New Deal. It would revive a more restrictive view of federal power, weaken regulation and return authority to the states. George Bush, who will never again face the electorate, may share that ambition. He certainly understands that his most enduring legacy could be written not in legislation but in judicial appointments.

Yet the outcome is not predetermined. Most Americans are decent and pragmatic people. They are often offended by the excesses of the right’s culture wars, as opinion polls repeatedly show and as the Terry Schiavo case recently reminded them. The louder activists may demand ideological purity. The wider public often prefers practical restraint.

Republican candidates facing the midterm elections may therefore be less enthusiastic about a culture-warrior nominee than the party’s pressure groups. They know that a bitter confirmation struggle could mobilise the left and repel the centre. Bush will also be under pressure to nominate another woman. His record suggests that, however much he sometimes panders to his party’s base, he does not entirely ignore the political middle ground. That was precisely the territory on which O’Connor’s cautious pragmatism played so successfully.

Now consider Britain. Here is a general knowledge question. Who is the newly appointed lord chief justice of England and Wales, taking over from Lord Woolf on October 1?

The answer is Nicholas Phillips, Lord Phillips of Worth Matravers, who has presided over the court of appeal for the past five years. Most readers will not know that. Nor will many know that Sir Anthony Clarke has been appointed master of the rolls, or that Sir Igor Judge – surely the best name on the English bench – is to become head of the Queen’s Bench Division.

These are three of the most important judicial offices in the country. Yet their new occupants were announced quietly in a press release containing almost no useful information about them. The appointments received cursory media attention. They appeared on no front pages. There was almost no follow-up coverage and no sustained public debate.

Were these the right people? What are their views on the major legal and constitutional issues of the day? What do their careers tell us about the direction in which they may take the courts? How do they understand the balance between liberty and security, parliament and the executive, judges and ministers? Such questions were barely asked, never mind answered.

The silence is both striking and eloquent. These were the first senior appointments made under the major changes introduced by the Constitutional Reform Act 2005. It might reasonably have been expected that they would be marked in a way that signalled to the public that these judges are key figures in a new constitutional era. In the event, nothing of the sort occurred. The most senior appointments in the supposedly reformed judicial system were made much as they always have been: unheralded, unexplained and with minimal openness.

Defenders of the British approach will say that this is how judicial independence is protected. Judges should be appointed on merit, not subjected to American-style partisan interrogation. There is force in that argument. No one sensible should wish to import the whole Washington confirmation circus, with its lobbying campaigns, attack advertisements, ritual evasions and ideological inquisitions.

But independence does not require obscurity. Merit does not require silence. Public accountability does not mean that judges should stand for election or promise particular verdicts. It means that citizens are entitled to know who their most powerful judges are, how they think about the law and what understanding of the constitution they bring to the bench.

Around $50m is reportedly expected to be spent in the battle over O’Connor’s seat. With two other justices in their 80s and a third suffering from cancer, the process may soon be repeated with still greater intensity, expense and epochal consequence. Seen from a distance, it can appear a travesty, even a corruption, of the selection of senior members of an independent, merit-based judiciary.

Yet the American process, for all its faults, explicitly and implicitly recognises the centrality of judges and law to modern democratic government. Britain, with rare and distinguished exceptions such as Lord Woolf, remains overwhelmingly in denial about these connections.

If citizens meekly concede that they have no right to know about the highest judges in the land, or to call their thinking to public account, they vacate an important part of the public arena. Judges deserve independence, but they must also earn respect. Unless we can respect judges, it is harder to respect the law. And unless we respect the law, it is harder to respect the system of government of which both law and judges are integral parts.

author: Martin Kettle
title: cross_topic_1/test/4/test_138
body:
There was a time when the American Republican party contained two recognisable conservative traditions. One was grand, interventionist and occasionally generous: the tradition of Theodore Roosevelt and, much later, Nelson Rockefeller, in which national strength could coexist with social obligation and public purpose. The other was more modest, managerial and cautious: the Republicanism of Dwight Eisenhower and, in a darker and more calculating form, Richard Nixon. Neither tradition was liberal. Neither should be romanticised. But both accepted that government involved practical limits, competing interests and obligations to people outside the party’s own tribe.

That Republican party has not entirely vanished. Political parties rarely vanish in so neat a fashion. Its voters can still be found in the suburbs, its voices occasionally heard in the Senate, its ghosts repeatedly summoned in convention speeches. Yet the party on display in New York in 2004 was a fundamentally different organism. It was an alliance between a dominant, aggressive and highly organised religious conservative activist wing and a much larger moderate electorate that still persuaded itself that the party was essentially benign.

This distinction matters. The Republican coalition is not made up solely of zealots. Most Americans who vote Republican are not zealots at all. They are decent, busy, pragmatic people who want lower taxes, safe streets, national confidence and a president who appears to know his own mind. Many think George Bush is a compassionate conservative because he says that he is one. They hear the reassuring code, but not always the harder message transmitted beneath it. The activists hear both.

The 2004 Republican national convention was therefore a revealing event, though not always in the way its organisers intended. On television it was packaged as a festival of moderation. New York was chosen as the setting. John McCain, Rudy Giuliani and Arnold Schwarzenegger were pushed towards the front of the stage. The message was diversity, tolerance, resolve and unity after September 11.

But conventions are not only what happens at the lectern in prime time. They are also what the delegates wear, applaud and shout. Beneath the moderate wrapping, the emotional engine of the gathering was hostility, and its principal target was John Kerry. Not disagreement with Kerry’s policies. Not even a sustained argument about his Senate record. Personal hostility was the point.

The most disgraceful example was the sight of delegates wearing Purple Heart band-aids to mock Kerry’s Vietnam wounds. Kerry had volunteered, served in combat and been decorated. George Bush had not fought in Vietnam. Dick Cheney had not fought there either, having obtained repeated deferments and later explaining, with an iciness that deserved to follow him through public life, that he had “other priorities”.

Yet it was Kerry whose courage was treated as fraudulent by a convention devoted to the re-election of Bush and Cheney. The platform speeches were scarcely less brutal. Zell Miller, the Democratic senator from Georgia turned Republican convention avenger, denounced Kerry as “wrong, weak and wobbly”. The hall roared its approval.

The hypocrisy is obvious, but hypocrisy is not the whole explanation. The point was not that Republicans revere military service so intensely that they had reluctantly concluded Kerry’s record was inadequate. If reverence had been the motive, the Purple Heart band-aids would have been unthinkable. The real purpose was to establish exclusive Republican ownership of defence and security politics. Military courage counted when it reinforced Republican claims to power. When it challenged them, it could be mocked.

This is why the convention contained criticism of intelligence agencies, Democrats, France, the United Nations, the media and the anti-war movement, but none of the military itself. There was no serious criticism of the chain of command over Abu Ghraib. The photographs from the prison were treated as an embarrassment to be contained, not as evidence requiring searching political or institutional accountability.

Imagine, for a moment, that Al Gore had been president on September 11 2001. Would Republicans have rallied around him as Democrats initially rallied around Bush? Some would. Most Americans behave better in a crisis than their political institutions encourage them to behave. But does anyone seriously believe that the Republican machine would have suspended partisan warfare? Or would Gore have been blamed for weakness, while Bill Clinton was accused of having allowed Osama bin Laden to escape?

A visit to a New York Barnes and Noble provided the answer. The shelves groaned with books whose titles and arguments assigned responsibility for September 11 backwards into the Clinton years: Losing Bin Laden, Dereliction of Duty, Intelligence Failure and many more in the same accusatory vein. The details differed. The underlying syllogism did not. America had been attacked; Democrats had recently governed America; therefore Democrats had enabled the attack.

This matters beyond the election. A President Kerry would not have faced an opposition chastened by defeat and eager for national cooperation. He would have faced a Republican movement convinced that Democratic possession of the White House was inherently illegitimate in an age of terror. Every setback would have become betrayal. Every intelligence dispute would have become scandal. The impeachment reflex, exercised against Clinton and never truly regretted, would have returned almost immediately.

Eisenhower’s warning against the “misplaced power” of the military-industrial complex is often quoted as though it were merely an interesting observation from a vanished cold-war world. In fact it reads increasingly like a description of the modern Republican party. The party is not simply supported by defence interests. It has become the political expression of a system in which military expenditure, corporate power, strategic anxiety and electoral advantage constantly reinforce one another.

The mood accompanying this system is militarist rather than merely military. Soldiers are disciplined professionals whose work is often difficult, restrained and tragic. Militarism is different. It is theatrical, emotional and politically useful. At the convention it was expressed in pounding music, clenched rhetoric and testosterone-driven chants of “U-S-A”. Strength was not presented as an instrument of policy. Strength was the policy.

Yet military-industrial power is only half the story. The modern Republican achievement has been to fuse it with religious imperatives. Bush’s own role in this depends on a technique that might be called double-coding. He uses language that sounds moderate, even vague, to secular or inattentive listeners, while carrying a precise meaning to the religious right. Words such as faith, life, freedom, evil, calling and culture operate on two levels at once.

To the moderate voter, they suggest character and decency. To the activist, they signal abortion, same-sex relationships, conservative judges, Christian nationalism and the larger project of restoring a supposedly lost moral order. Bush does not need to spell out every promise. The movement understands what it is being told.

Sometimes, though, the coding breaks down and the underlying message emerges in full. At a private rally during convention week, Senator Sam Brownback of Kansas declared that America was engaged in a “culture war”. This was not metaphorical decoration. It was a statement of political purpose. A culture war requires enemies, loyalty and victory. It does not encourage the modest accommodations on which plural democracy depends.

The same note sounded at the convention’s closing prayer, which asked for guidance against the “forces of evil”. Prayers need not be politically neutral, and evil unquestionably exists. But in this setting the phrase did not float free of the week’s partisan passions. It came after opponents had been derided as weak, treacherous and unfit to defend the nation. The boundary between spiritual evil and political opposition had been left ominously indistinct.

Much of the convention was Orwellian double-speak. Aggression was called compassion. Division was called unity. Recklessness was called resolve. A party dominated by ideological activists presented itself through moderate mascots to an electorate it hoped would not look too closely behind the scenery.

But the most troubling words were not the coded ones. They were the plain ones: culture war, forces of evil, wrong, weak and wobbly. They require no elaborate decoding at all. They should be taken seriously precisely because they mean what they say.

author: Martin Kettle
title: cross_topic_1/train/4/train_34
body:
Since the Victorian era there have been three great electoral landslides of the British left. There was New Labour’s victory in 1997, repeated with scarcely diminished force in 2001. There was Labour’s triumph under Clement Attlee in 1945. And there was the Liberal landslide of 1906, one of the most sweeping and consequential election victories in our history. The first was won by Tony Blair, the second by Attlee, and the third by a man almost no one remembers.

His name was Sir Henry Campbell-Bannerman. He was known to his contemporaries, with an informality that now seems almost exotic, simply as CB. A century or so ago he became prime minister and launched the great Liberal era in government. That era lasted, through war, political upheaval and changes of leadership, for nearly 17 years, until the fall of the Lloyd George coalition in 1922. No other British government of the left has remained continuously in office for so long.

Yet Campbell-Bannerman has almost vanished from the national memory. The Liberal governments of the period are associated above all with Asquith and Lloyd George, with the People’s Budget, the Parliament Act and the origins of the welfare state. But the pivotal victory that made all those things possible belonged not to Asquith or Lloyd George. It belonged to CB alone.

It is easy to see why he was underestimated. Margot Asquith described him as “essentially a bon vivant, a boulevardier and a humorist”. Such qualities have rarely been prominent in British prime ministers since Disraeli, and they have become rarer still in the modern age of organised earnestness. His biographer John Wilson portrayed him as easy-going, deficient in personal ambition and inclined, where possible, to let well alone.

All this is true, up to a point. CB kept politics in proportion. He was not a workaholic and did not pretend to be one. He enjoyed food, conversation, France and books. He did not treat every disagreement as a betrayal or every opponent as an enemy. Most remarkably of all, perhaps, he did not bear grudges. In an occupation populated by men and women who can remember a slight for 30 years, this alone marks him out.

But it would be a mistake to confuse his good nature with laziness, or his political flexibility with an absence of belief. Campbell-Bannerman was not an intellectual politician. He had no systematic political philosophy and made no claim to one. Yet his Liberal convictions were deep, instinctive and unwavering, rather like his lifelong attachment to Balzac and Flaubert.

In an 1898 speech he defined Liberalism as faith in self-government, “trust in the general sense of mankind”, hostility to artificial privilege, and a determination that the laws should not aggravate inequality but should secure to every person “a chance of a good and useful life”. More than a century later, that still reads well. Indeed, it reads rather better than much of the evasive political language manufactured today.

There are three large reasons why Campbell-Bannerman deserves rescue from oblivion. The first was his stand over the Boer war. The conflict came close to splitting the Liberal party. Much of the party hierarchy, gathered around Lord Rosebery, supported the war or shrank from opposing it. The radicals denounced it as an imperial crime. As the new leader, CB initially sought to hold the middle ground between the two camps.

But holding the middle ground did not mean sitting permanently on the fence. In 1901 Campbell-Bannerman made the most important anti-war speech of the era. He condemned the Salisbury government’s conduct of the campaign, including the burning of farms and the concentration camps, as “methods of barbarism”. The phrase caused outrage. It was attacked as unpatriotic and disloyal to British troops. Yet it was morally exact, politically brave and historically vindicated. At a moment when many around him preferred ambiguity, CB found the necessary words.

The second reason was his pragmatic radicalism towards the emerging Labour party. Campbell-Bannerman liked Labour politicians, which was not something that could be said of every senior Liberal. He sympathised with their grievances and understood that organised labour had become a permanent force in democratic politics. He neither romanticised Labour nor treated it as an invading army.

Within months of his taking office, the government passed the Trades Disputes Act of 1906. Asquith tried to weaken the measure. Campbell-Bannerman resisted him, backed the stronger version and pushed it through the cabinet. The act protected trade unions from actions for damages arising from strikes and became the cornerstone of 20th-century labour law. This was not merely amiable pragmatism. It was a decisive use of power on behalf of working people.

The third reason is the simplest and, in conventional political terms, perhaps the strongest. Campbell-Bannerman was very good at politics. He led from the centre of his party, not from one of its armed encampments. After the heavy Liberal defeat of 1900, he held the party together, restored its confidence and exploited the growing divisions among its opponents.

The scale of the achievement is astonishing. A Conservative majority of 134 was transformed into a Liberal majority of 130. It was one of the greatest electoral swings in British history. CB achieved it by concentrating on the weaknesses of the government, especially the protectionist tariff campaign, while avoiding extravagant promises of his own. Most of his senior colleagues thought the strategy inadequate. The voters thought otherwise.

Nor did his government disappoint once it took office. On the contrary, it outperformed expectations. If the Conservative-dominated House of Lords had not obstructed its programme, Campbell-Bannerman might have secured education reform, abolition of the multiple franchise and trade union law reform in his first year alone. Even so, the government contained ministers who proved themselves genuine reformers. Haldane transformed the army. Lloyd George reformed merchant shipping. The administration began to show that Liberal government could mean more than retrenchment and good intentions.

Enoch Powell famously observed that all political careers end in failure. The line was written about Joseph Chamberlain, whose last great campaign, for tariff reform, helped to deliver Campbell-Bannerman’s landslide. But the axiom does not really apply to CB, except in the literal sense that his career ended because his life did.

He died in Downing Street in April 1908. A few weeks earlier, gravely ill and confined to bed, he had resigned the premiership in favour of Asquith. There was no repudiation at the polls, no cabinet coup, no humiliating removal and no long decline into querulous memoir-writing. Campbell-Bannerman went out at the top, the architect of a landslide and the leader of a government whose greatest achievements still lay ahead.

Why, then, is he so forgotten? Andrew Bonar Law was once the obvious rival for the title of Britain’s most obscure prime minister, until Robert Blake rescued him in a celebrated biography appropriately called The Unknown Prime Minister. Alec Douglas-Home may one day retreat still further into the shadows as the world that produced him becomes increasingly remote.

For the moment, though, the title belongs elsewhere. It belongs, unfairly and unnecessarily, to the genial, principled, radical and politically skilful man who made the Liberal century possible but did not live to see its greatest battles. Britain’s unknown prime minister is the admirable and neglected Henry Campbell-Bannerman.

author: Martin Kettle
title: cross_topic_1/train/4/train_37
body:
On the morning of the 1983 general election, I fell into conversation with a Labour ward secretary in Hackney. What did I think would happen, he asked. The Conservatives would win by more than 100 seats, I replied. Nonsense, he said. Labour would win by 20. He knew the streets, the activists and the voters. I merely knew what I had read. Yet Margaret Thatcher was returned that night with a majority of 144.

Fourteen years later, on election day in 1997, I asked the Labour leader what result he expected. Perhaps Labour would win by 50, he said, with the caution of a man who had lived too long with his party’s gift for defeat to trust the approaching triumph. More like 150, I replied. Labour’s eventual majority was 179.

There was nothing mystical about either judgment. I possessed no private exit poll, no constituency whispering network and no psephological philosopher’s stone. My secret, if that is not too grand a word for so prosaic a habit, was simply that I believed what the opinion polls said.

This is still treated in some circles as evidence of innocence bordering on folly. Polls, one is told, are always wrong. Or they are manipulated. Or they merely tell their commissioners what they want to hear. The pollsters missed 1992, didn’t they? And what about this survey, which says one thing, and that survey, which says something else? To place trust in polls, in this account, is to surrender judgment to a mysterious priesthood armed with clipboards, telephone numbers and decimal points.

Yet most of the time the polls are not wrong at all. Most of the time, indeed, they are impressively right. They may not always tell us what we want to hear. They may sometimes tell us something we do not properly understand. But these are not the same thing as unreliability.

When I am asked now who is winning the American presidential election on November 2, my answer is John Kerry. This is not because I want Kerry to win, though I do. Nor is it because, as some of my more hostile rightwing American email correspondents insist with their customary charm, I am an anti-American socialist appeaser, a Euro-weenie or merely an idiot. It is because, at the moment, that is what the polls say.

The national surveys are extremely close. More importantly, since American presidents are elected state by state through the electoral college rather than by a single national vote, Kerry is doing narrowly better in enough of the battleground states to justify saying that he is ahead. If the election were being held now, and if the polls were exactly translated into votes, Kerry would probably win.

But note all the qualifications in that sentence. Saying Kerry is ahead in the polls is not the same as predicting that Kerry will win the election. Polls are snapshots, not prophecies. They have margins of error. Different polling organisations use different methods. Turnout assumptions matter. The election is still months away. There will be conventions, debates, advertisements, world events, economic figures, blunders and perhaps scandals before America votes. All one can responsibly say now is that the contest is close and that Kerry is narrowly ahead.

Poll-believers nevertheless have some explaining to do this week. Two recent British polls appear, at first sight, to point in wholly different directions about the political importance of Iraq. A Guardian-ICM poll put Iraq at the bottom of a list of issues likely to decide how people vote at the next general election. Yet an FT-Mori survey found defence and foreign affairs to be the most important issue facing the country.

What is the ordinary reader supposed to make of that? Are foreign affairs simultaneously the most important issue and the least important one? Can Iraq dominate public argument and yet barely register at election time? Respected observers, including Peter Preston, have understandably confessed themselves confused by the apparent contradiction.

But the confusion is more apparent than real. And this matters, not merely for pollsters or political anoraks but for the civic good. The lazy conceit that polls cannot be trusted is corrosive. So is the equally lazy claim that they can be made to say anything. Both encourage the comforting belief that evidence may be dismissed whenever it proves inconvenient. It is worth stamping them out.

The resolution is straightforward. The two polls asked different questions.

ICM gave respondents a prompted list of subjects and asked which would be most important in deciding how they voted. Only 12% selected Iraq. The NHS came top, chosen by 59%. This was a question about the considerations people expect to bring into the polling booth when choosing between parties.

Mori asked an unprompted question about the most important issue facing Britain today. No list was read out. Respondents answered in their own words. “Defence/foreign affairs/international terrorism” came first, mentioned by 24% as the single most important issue and by 38% when other important issues were included.

These findings are not contradictory. They are compatible. They may even be mutually illuminating. It is perfectly possible to think that terrorism and foreign affairs constitute the gravest issue facing the nation while not giving them much weight in deciding whether to vote Labour, Conservative or Liberal Democrat.

This is not an esoteric distinction. Ask someone what is the most important problem facing the world and they may answer climate change, nuclear proliferation, global poverty or religious conflict. Ask the same person what matters most in deciding how to vote and they may answer the local hospital, school standards, council tax or whether they trust the government with the economy. Neither answer invalidates the other. They address different questions.

Nor is this a new phenomenon. Throughout the 1980s, polls regularly found unemployment to be the most important issue facing the country. They also regularly showed that Labour was thought to have the best policies for dealing with it. Yet the Conservatives won election after election.

Why? Not because the polls were wrong. They accurately recorded both public anxiety about unemployment and Labour’s comparative strength on the issue. But elections were not decided by asking which party was best on the subject that voters considered the country’s largest problem in the abstract. They were decided by a bundle of more immediate judgments: who would keep taxes down, who could be trusted to run the economy, who looked competent, who represented stability and who would leave the voter and the voter’s family better off. On those voting-day questions the Conservatives held the advantage.

Something similar is happening now. Iraq, international terrorism and Britain’s relations with the wider world loom enormously over public life. They dominate newspapers, television bulletins and parliamentary debate. They shape attitudes towards Tony Blair. They may even be the issues by which history eventually judges this government. None of that means they will determine the next general election.

When voters come to choose a government, the questions become more personal and more practical. What has happened to the NHS? Are schools improving? Is the economy secure? Can the mortgage be paid? Are public services better? Which party seems competent, and which alternative seems ready? These are not necessarily nobler questions than war and peace. But they are the questions on which governments are usually made and unmade.

This does not mean Iraq is electorally irrelevant. If events deteriorate sharply, or if distrust of Blair becomes a general judgment on Labour’s competence and honesty, Iraq may infect every other issue. Polls are snapshots, here as in America. Things can change. Yet the evidence currently suggests that most voters separate their view of Iraq from their choice of government more than Westminster sometimes imagines.

That is the real lesson of the two surveys. The polls are not failing to describe public opinion. They are describing its complexity. The contradiction lies not in the findings but in the assumption that voters can hold only one hierarchy of concerns at a time.

Foreign affairs and terrorism may indeed be the most important issues facing Britain. But elections are fought and decided on health, education, prosperity and the texture of personal life. On those voting-day issues Labour retains the advantage. That is why Labour will win the next election. Just as the polls say it will.

author: Martin Kettle
title: cross_topic_1/train/4/train_39
body:
It is one of the hardest tests in politics for true believers to criticise publicly the causes they hold dear. The sceptic risks little by being sceptical. The loyalist risks identity, friendship and faith. He must admit not merely that a policy has failed, but that the people and institutions in which he has invested his judgment may not deserve the confidence he placed in them. Conscience and loyalty are therefore perpetually at war. Neither wins easily. Loyalty without conscience becomes servility; conscience without loyalty can become vanity.

Tony Blair’s belief in the importance of the United States alliance is a classic case of the perils of undifferentiated loyalty. His present difficulties over Iraq are not simply tactical troubles arising from one difficult crisis. They reflect a deeper struggle between enduring instincts and threatening realities. Blair wants to believe that, by standing close to Washington, Britain can influence American power in a moral and internationalist direction. The evidence increasingly suggests that the administration to which he has attached himself does not accept the rules by which Blair imagines the relationship operating.

Blair’s attachment to America began, in part, as a view about British domestic politics. He has long thought that politics here operates “downstream” from politics in the United States. The ideological weather systems form across the Atlantic before arriving, somewhat weakened but still unmistakable, on British shores. Bill Clinton’s victory in 1992 was therefore more than an encouraging result for Democrats. It was, in Blair’s view, one of the preconditions for Labour’s own recovery. Clinton showed that a centre-left party could embrace economic change, talk about opportunity rather than class, and win.

Clinton’s re-election in 1996 mattered almost as much. It made Labour’s impending victory in 1997 appear aligned with the grain of history rather than a belated national aberration. New Labour did not look like an isolated European social-democratic government arriving after the age of the left had ended. It looked like the British expression of a wider modernising movement whose principal headquarters happened to be the White House.

George W Bush’s election in 2000 thus posed Blair a serious political problem. The American tide had changed direction. If British politics really was downstream, a Republican presidency might eventually strengthen the Conservatives and make Labour appear stranded. This helps to explain Blair’s haste to become the first foreign visitor to Camp David in early 2001. The visit was about diplomacy, certainly, and about learning to deal with an unfamiliar president. But it was also about domestic positioning. Blair was determined that William Hague, and later Iain Duncan Smith, should not claim that the Conservatives were now the British party naturally in touch with the new America.

Yet Blair’s belief in the alliance widened from domestic politics into a doctrine of international affairs. The United States, in this view, is the indispensable nation. No grave global problem can be settled without it. Europe may supply law, money, diplomacy and legitimacy, but only America can supply the decisive weight. The Anglo-American relationship is therefore not merely a sentimental inheritance. It is the catalyst that keeps America engaged in the world rather than allowing it to retreat into isolationism or unilateral force.

There has always been an ambiguity here. How much does Blair truly believe in the full mystique of the “special relationship”, with all its Churchillian furnishings, and how much does he merely regard it as useful statecraft? He is, after all, evidently and sincerely a European. But there are moments when the heart speaks more clearly than the policy paper. At a White House dinner with Clinton in 1998, Blair quoted the words used by Harry Hopkins to Churchill during the second world war: “Whither thou goest I will go… Thy people shall be my people, and thy God my God. Even to the end.” That was more than diplomatic decoration. It revealed where Blair’s emotional loyalties lay.

Even under Clinton, of course, Blair sometimes struggled to secure American engagement. Kosovo did not produce instant agreement or effortless leadership. The indispensable nation could be reluctant, distracted and difficult. But Bush presents a fundamentally different problem. The issue is no longer simply how to persuade an internationalist administration to act internationally. It is whether the administration accepts internationalism’s basic premises at all.

Writing in the New York Review of Books, Frances Fitzgerald observed that the Bush administration broke with the internationalist assumptions accepted by every postwar American administration except Reagan’s first. That judgment goes to the heart of Blair’s predicament. Bush has rarely defined his foreign-policy objectives in concrete and limited terms. Instead he speaks of dangers, enemies and security solutions whose meaning is determined by the United States itself. During the campaign he offered the revealing remark: “We’re not so sure who the they are, but we know they’re there.”

This is not merely presidential imprecision. Bush’s most powerful subordinates have turned vagueness into doctrine. Dick Cheney and Donald Rumsfeld approach alliances as temporary instruments rather than reciprocal obligations. John Bolton states the underlying proposition with unusual candour. “There is no such thing as the UN,” he has said. The international community, in this account, is not a community at all. It is what the United States can lead when leadership happens to suit American interests.

The same logic shapes military planning. The Pentagon has shifted from a “threat-based strategy” to a “capabilities-based approach”. The phrase sounds technical, perhaps even prudent. Its significance is political. A threat-based strategy requires the government to identify an enemy, estimate the danger and justify the resources needed to meet it. A capabilities-based approach asks what hostile power might conceivably do, then demands the capacity to defeat it. Rumsfeld speaks of defending “against the unknown”. But the unknown has no budgetary limit. It cannot be disproved, negotiated with or finally defeated. It is the perfect justification for permanent military expansion.

All this constantly undermines Blair’s effort to portray the Bush administration as a willing partner in the moral internationalist order he championed in his Brighton conference speech and reiterated to the TUC. Blair speaks of international law, collective responsibility and the duty to act against intolerable regimes. Washington speaks the same language when useful, but reserves the right to discard it when inconvenient. Blair treats partnership as a principle. The administration treats it as an option.

One should retain some sympathy for Blair. He believes in many of the right things. America does need to be engaged. Britain does need influence in Washington. Saddam Hussein’s Iraq is not a problem invented by Republican ideologues. It is easier to mock Blair’s effort than to propose a convincing alternative capable of restraining American power while also dealing with Iraqi defiance.

But Blair earns grief both at home and in Washington because there is a profound disjunction between what he wants to believe about this administration and what is actually true. At home, his loyalty looks like submission. In Washington, his internationalism looks like an irritating demand for permission and company. He pays the price in both capitals because he will not acknowledge the contradiction.

The administration has discarded the rules by which Blair wants to play. It does not share his conception of alliances, institutions or international order. Yet rather than confront this reality, Blair pretends it does not exist. That is the classic error of the over-loyalist. He imagines that fidelity gives him influence, when it may merely deprive him of judgment. Conscience and loyalty will always contend in political leadership. But when loyalty overrides clarity, truth is usually the first casualty and sound policy the next.
